title: Reduction in Uterine and Fibroid Volumes in 135 Consecutive Subjects Following Laparoscopic and Ultrasound-Guided Radiofrequency Ablation of Fibroids: 12-Month Follow-Up
authors_raw: R.S. Guido, D.J. Levine, D.I. Galen, J.A. Macer, ... S.G. Chudnoff
title_normalized: reduction in uterine and fibroid volumes in 135 consecutive subjects following laparoscopic and ultrasound guided radiofrequency ablation of fibroids 12 month follow up
abstract_text: Study Objective: To assess outpatient radiofrequency volumetric thermal ablation (RFVTA) in women with symptomatic fibroids and moderate-to-severe menorrhagia, in terms of uterine and fibroid volume reduction (measured by contrast-enhanced MRI) and incidence of surgical re-intervention within 12 months posttreatment. Design: Prospective, multicenter, longitudinal, single-arm, paired-comparison interventional clinical trial involving 135 participants. Setting: Nine university teaching hospitals and private treatment sites throughout the United States and 2 university teaching hospitals in Latin America. Patients: 135 women (ages 30 to 54 years) with moderate-to-severe menorrhagia (≥ 160 mL to ≤ 500 mL). Intervention: Laparoscopic and ultrasound-guided RFVTA. Measurements and Main Results: Of 818 fibroids identified on intraoperative ultrasound, 674 fibroids were treated by RFVTA (mean: 5.0 ± 4.28 fibroids per patient). Mean uterine volume decreased by 15.8% (n = 126) and 25.1% (n = 127), respectively, at 3 months and 12 months postprocedure from the mean baseline value of 361.3 ± 193.1 cm3 (n = 135) (p < .0001). Mean fibroid volume decreased by 37.7% (n = 121) and 44.3% (n = 118), respectively, at 3- and 12-months postprocedure from the mean baseline fibroid volume of 80.9 ± Conclusion: RFVTA is an effective outpatient treatment for women with symptomatic fibroids including moderate-to-severe menorrhagia resulting in statistically significant reduction of fibroid and uterine volumes and clinically significant reduction in menstrual bleeding.

title: Prospective 12-Month Follow-Up of Menstrual Blood Loss Reduction Following 135 Consecutive Cases of Radiofrequency Volumetric Thermal Ablation of Symptomatic Fibroids
authors_raw: E. Banks, M. Harris, J Garza-Leal, R. Robles Pemueller, ... J.M. Berman
title_normalized: prospective 12 month follow up of menstrual blood loss reduction following 135 consecutive cases of radiofrequency volumetric thermal ablation of symptomatic fibroids
abstract_text: Study Objective: To assess the efficacy and safety of radiofrequency volumetric thermal ablation (RFVTA) in women with symptomatic fibroids and moderate-to-severe menorrhagia, in terms of mean menstrual blood loss, incidence of device-related adverse events, and surgical re-intervention. Design: Prospective, multicenter, longitudinal, single-arm, paired-comparison interventional clinical trial involving 135 participants. Setting: Nine university teaching hospitals and private treatment sites throughout the United States and two university teaching hospitals in Latin America. Patients: 135 women (ages 30 to 54 years) with moderate-to-severe menorrhagia (≥ 160 mL to ≤ 500 mL) as verified by alkaline hematin (AH) testing. Intervention: Laparoscopic and ultrasound-guided RFVTA. Measurements and Main Results: The mean AH at baseline was 271.5 ± 80.67 mL. A total of 674 fibroids (mean per subject 5.0 ± 4.28, median 4.0, range: 1–29) between 1.0 cm and 9.7 cm in diameter of various types (193 subserosals, 347 intramurals, 39 transmurals, and 164 submucosals) were treated by RFVTA. At 3 months posttreatment, 124 subjects had a mean AH of 188.0 ± 139.19 mL—a reduction of 31% (p < .0001). At 6 months posttreatment, 128 subjects had a mean AH of 160.7 ± 101.67 mL—a reduction of 41% from baseline (p < Conclusion: RFVTA is safe and effective for the treatment of moderate-to-severe menorrhagia in women with symptomatic fibroids and provides clinically and statistically significant reduction in menstrual blood loss.

title: The Impact of Robot Acquisition on Method of Hysterectomy at a Single Institution
authors_raw: K.N. Wright, A. DiSciuillo, P. Rosenblatt
title_normalized: the impact of robot acquisition on method of hysterectomy at a single institution
abstract_text: Study Objective: To observe the impact of the acquisition of a surgical robotic system on method of hysterectomy performed in a gynecology department. Design: Retrospective cohort study. Chi-squared tests were used where appropriate. Setting: Medium-sized academic community hospital. Patients: 1111 women who underwent a hysterectomy from 2006 to April 10, 2012. Intervention: Total and supracervical abdominal, vaginal, laparoscopic, and robotic hysterectomies. Acquisition of a surgical robot in early 2011. Measurements and Main Results: 1111 hysterectomies were performed in the time period studied. From 2006 to 2010, abdominal hysterectomy decreased from 26% to 8% (p<.001) while laparoscopic hysterectomy increased from 59% to 82% (p<.001) of all cases. Vaginal hysterectomy did not change significantly (15 vs. 10%, p = 0.90). After addition of the robot in early 2011, laparoscopic hysterectomy decreased to 64%, vaginal hysterectomy decreased to 7%, and abdominal hysterectomy increased to 10% (p<.001). 19% of hysterectomies that Conclusion: Acquisition of a robot decreases laparoscopic hysterectomy while keeping abdominal hysterectomy the same. Though the robotic manufacturer claims that the robot decreases abdominal hysterectomy, we found this was not true for our institution.

title: Hysterectomy Complications among Overweight and Obese Women: Role of 95% Confidence Intervals
authors_raw: K.A. Bernhard, D.S. Siddiqui, H.A. Louks, S.P. Chauhan
title_normalized: hysterectomy complications among overweight and obese women role of 95 confidence intervals
abstract_text: Study Objective: Surgical complications for surgeons are reported as rate to permit transparency and informed decision. We sought to evaluate the importance of utilizing 95% confidence intervals (CI) in reporting overall complication rate of surgeons performing benign hysterectomies in overweight (BMI≥25 kg/m2) and obese (BMI ≥30) patients. Design: Surgeons who performed a hysterectomy from July 2007 to December 2011 on women with BMI ≥25. Exclusion criteria: hysterectomy performed for malignant and obstetrical indications. For each surgeon, the overall complication rate, surgical, and medical complication rates were calculated along with 95% CI. Non-overlapping 95% CI were considered significant. Setting: Aurora Sinai Medical Center, Milwaukee, WI. Patients: 25 surgeons met the inclusion criteria. Measurements and Main Results: During the four-year study period, 538 hysterectomies were performed. The cohort of surgeons had an overall complication rate of 25% (95% CI: 21-28%), surgical complication rate of 9% (95% CI: 6-11%), and medical complication rate of 12% (95% CI: 10-15%). For the 25 surgeons, the rate of overall complication was 0 -100%, surgical complication 0-50%, and medical complication 0-100%. Comparisons of 95% CI indicated that one surgeon had significantly higher overall complications (34%; 95% CI: Conclusion: At a tertiary hospital, for an overwhelming majority, the rates of complications are similar among gynecologists. Reporting the 95% CI, as opposed to other statistical analyses, allows for greater transparency due to its simplicity and convenience. This conservative method is feasible to differentiate the few who have significantly higher and lower complications allowing for a preliminary analysis before a surgical procedure.

title: Perioperative Outcomes of Robot Assisted Laparoscopic Hysterectomy Using a Retroperitoneal Approach
authors_raw: K.E. Hew, R. MacDonald, D.D. Im
title_normalized: perioperative outcomes of robot assisted laparoscopic hysterectomy using a retroperitoneal approach
abstract_text: Study Objective: To describe robot assisted hysterectomy using a retroperitoneal approach and compare the perioperative outcomes with the traditional approach. Design: Quasi-Experimental Before and After Study. Setting: Mercy Medical Center, Baltimore, Maryland. Patients: 198 patients underwent scheduled robot assisted laparoscopic hysterectomy for benign indications between July 2010 and January 2012. 101 patients underwent robot assisted hysterectomy using the traditional approach and 97 using the retroperitoneal approach. Intervention: Robot assisted laparoscopic hysterectomy using a retroperitoneal approach for benign indications was evaluated through a retrospective chart review and compared with the traditional method. Measurements and Main Results: Data collected included age, race, body mass index (BMI), medical comorbidities, number of prior abdominal surgeries, pathology and uterine weight. Variables for perioperative outcomes included total surgery length, estimated blood loss (EBL), intraoperative and post operative complications and total hospital charges. The two groups were similar with respect to demographic data, BMI, medical comorbidities, number of previous abdominal surgeries, and type of pathology. With the traditional Conclusion: Robot assisted hysterectomy using a retroperitoneal approach is an alternate method to the traditional method with comparable perioperative outcomes. There was a trend towards decreased post operative complications. However, larger randomized prospective studies need to be performed to further evaluate this approach.

title: Prospective Cohort Study of Bowel Function after Robotic Sacrocolpopexy
authors_raw: C.M. Lewis, C. Salamon, E. Gurshumov, J. Priestley, P. Culligan
title_normalized: prospective cohort study of bowel function after robotic sacrocolpopexy
abstract_text: Study Objective: To determine changes in bowel function after robotic sacrocolpopexy. Design: A single-center prospective cohort study from 2007-2011. Data were collected at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months postoperatively. The primary outcome measure was the Colorectal-anal Distress Inventory, Short Form 8 (CRADI-8) and the Colorectal-anal Impact Questionnaire, Short Form 7 (CRAIQ-7). Our secondary outcome measure was “splinting for defecation”. In addition, using the difference between baseline and 12 month CRADI-8 scores, we created three groups (‘improved', ‘unchanged’, and Setting: Tertiary-care hospital in New Jersey. Patients: 432 patients with symptomatic prolapse. Intervention: Robotic-assisted sacrocolpopexy. Measurements and Main Results: Of the 423 patients who underwent robotic sacrocolpopexy, 12 month follow up was available for 393 (93%). Mean CRADI-8 scores improved significantly from 21.1 ± 20 at baseline to 8.3 ± 12 at 6 months (p<0.0001) and 7.2 ± 11 at 12 months (p<0.0001). Similarly, CRAIQ-7 scores improved significantly from 11.1 ± 20 at baseline to 3.7 ± 13 at 6 months (p<0.0001) and 3.1 ± 11 at 12 months (p<0.0001). Preoperatively, 39% complained of splinting on the vagina or perineum to complete a bowel movement. Conclusion: Following robotic sacrocolpopexy, 94% of patients reported ‘improved’ or ‘unchanged’ bowel function.

title_normalized: robot assisted hysterectomies for advanced endometriosis
abstract_text: Study Objective: Laparoscopic surgery is considered the gold standard treatment of endometriosis when conservative therapy fails. Advanced endometriosis (AE), characterized by nodular disease and cul-de-sac obliteration, poses significant challenges for the laparoscopic approach. Although robot-assisted laparoscopy offers several technical advantages over traditional laparoscopy when treating complex pathology, lack of haptic feedback is considered one drawback of robotic technology that may limit its utility Design: Retrospective chart review. Setting: Tertiary teaching hospital. Patients: Consecutive women who underwent RAH for stage III (12 women) and stage IV (38 women) endometriosis between March 2002 and May 2010. Intervention: Robot-assisted total (TLH) or supracervical (LSH) hysterectomy. Measurements and Main Results: Fifty women underwent RAH during the study period, the majority of whom (76.0%) were Caucasian. The mean age and BMI were 40.5 ± 5.7 years and 27.5 ± 6.0 kg/m2, respectively. Forty-four women underwent TLH and 6 women underwent LSH. Eighty-eight percent of women underwent unilateral (34.0%) or bilateral salpingo-oopherectomies (54.0%), while 60.0% required extensive ureterolysis. Mean skin-to-dressing time was 235.9 ± 67.8 minutes and estimated blood loss was 162.0 ± 205.3 milliliters. The mean Conclusion: RAH for the treatment of AE has acceptable intraoperative and post-operative complications, and may be considered as an additional minimally invasive approach for treatment of these complex patients.

title: Use of Trendelenburg Position in Robotic Surgery: Can Less Be More?
authors_raw: K.L. Sasada, L.S. Mihalov
title_normalized: use of trendelenburg position in robotic surgery can less be more
abstract_text: Study Objective: To compare surgical console times, estimated blood loss (EBL), body mass index (BMI) and uterine weight for daVinci robotic assisted benign total laparoscopic hysterectomy, with or without salpingo-oophorectomy in patients placed in maximum Trendelenburg position (30° head-down) versus minimum Trendelenburg position (<20°). Design: Retrospective Chart Review. Setting: Tertiary Referral Center. Patients: 50 women undergoing benign robotic assisted hysterectomy by one surgeon from October 2010 to February 2012. Intervention: Using a clinometer to measure minimum necessary degree of Trendelenburg. Measurements and Main Results: Patients were positioned in the dorsal lithotomy position and placed either in maximum Trendelenburg position or minimum amount as deemed by the surgeon to safely complete the surgery. Minimum Trendelenburg position was recorded using a clinometer and was defined as less than 20 degrees. Surgeon time at console was recorded and compared between the two groups. Mean console time for the maximum Trendelenburg groups was 79.1 minutes, mean time for minimum Trendelenburg group was 66.5, p-value of Conclusion: Operating times are not significantly longer when using a minimum degree of Trendelenburg position. Therefore, minimum degree of T-burg position can be as effective in achieving adequate surgical exposure without increasing operative time.

Data included age, BMI, co-morbid conditions, previous abdominal surgeries, operative complications, (LOS), uterine weights and surgical indications and findings. Cost comparisons included surgical/room time and total patient care cost. TAH patients more likely to undergo hysterectomy for fibroids (p = 0.04) and anemia (p = 0.01). TAH group has significantly more EBL (p = 0.001) and longer LOS (p = 0.001). Intra and post operative complications were significantly higher with TAH (p = 0.001). RAH group had more patients with dysmenorrhea (p = 0.01) and no Conclusion: In both groups uterine weights up to 500 grams had no significant difference in surgical and total OR time. Uterine weight >500 grams surgical and OR time was longer in RAH group. Opertive compliactions combined were significantly higher in TAH group. Costs are similar for both groups. title: Blinded Measure of Trendelenburg Angle in Pelvic Robotic Surgery
authors_raw: C.H. Gould, B. Osmundsen, Y.X. Wu, T. Cull
title_normalized: blinded measure of trendelenburg angle in pelvic robotic surgery
abstract_text: Study Objective: To estimate the degree of Trendelenburg needed to perform robotic gynecologic surgeries. Design: Cross sectional study. Setting: Community hospital. Patients: All women undergoing gynecologic pelvic surgery using the daVinci surgical robot at a single institution between December 2010 and April 2011. Intervention: The primary surgeon determined the angle of Trendelenburg needed for adequate visualization to perform surgery defined as small bowel and sigmoid colon displaced out of the surgical field. Measurements and Main Results: The primary outcome, measured in a blinded fashion, was the degree of Trendelenburg positioning needed to complete the surgical procedure robotically. Secondary data points collected included body mass index, type of surgery performed, maximum end tidal CO2 and maximum peak inspiratory pressure. Main Results: Sixteen surgeons performed a total of 104 robotic gynecologic pelvic surgeries during our study period. Data was available on 86 cases. The mean Trendelenburg angle used in this study was 28.0 degrees(95% C.I. 26.9-29.1). This was significantly less than a historical maximum of 40 degrees (p < .0001). The degree of Trendelenburg used did not correlate with BMI (r = -0.2, p = .131) or the type of surgery performed (p = .41). Neither the maximum end tidal CO2 or maximum peak inspiratory pressure Conclusion: A mean Trendelenburg angle of 28.0 degrees was adequate to complete most gynecologic robotics cases compared to historical control angle of 40 degrees.

authors_raw: J.Q. Huang, M. Frey, J. Kofinas
title_normalized: comparison of perioperative outcomes associated with robotic assisted hysterectomy and myomectomy
abstract_text: Study Objective: To compare the perioperative outcomes in patients undergoing robotic - assisted hysterectomy (RAH) and robotic - assisted myomectomy (RAM) performed by a single surgeon. Design: Retrospective analysis. Setting: University - affiliated community hospital. Patients: Using the New York Hospital Queens surgical database, we identified patients who underwent either RAH or RAM from May 2010 to March 2012. We examined the relationship between the patients' perioperative outcomes and the type of surgery performed (RAH versus RAM). Intervention: Robotic-assisted myomectomy and robotic-assisted hysterectomy. Measurements and Main Results: We identified 199 RAH patients and 100 RAM patients during the study period. Patients who underwent RAM were found to be significantly younger and more likely to be nulliparous. We found decreased operative time (112min vs. 129min, p = 0.03) and similar blood loss in the RAH group despite larger uterine size (246gm vs 148gm, p < 0.0001). The length of hospital stay was similar in the two groups. We then performed a separate analysis for patients with large uteri, defined as specimen weighing Conclusion: Robotic hysterectomy is associated with shorter operative time and decreased blood loss, especially in patients with large uteri. While both RAM and RAH are safe surgical modalities for treatment of symptomatic leiomyomas, we should continue to recommend RAH to patients who no longer desire fertility preservation.

The surgical approach in this study did not confer any significant advantage for the long term outcomes studied.

abstract_text: Study Objective: Determine the effect of using barbed sutures on operating room (OR) time and overall surgery cost in robotic hysterectomy. Design: Premier Perspective Database was used to identify all inpatient hospital discharges with a robotic-assistance hysterectomy procedure (ICD-9-CM code, 68.31, 68.41, 68.61 and 17.x) between January 1, 2009 and June 30, 2010. Patients were further classified into two groups: 1) barbed suture use, 2) traditional suture group Propensity score methodology was used to match (1:1) the two groups using patient's age, race, severity of illness and hospital's region, bed size and teaching status. Setting: Inpatient discharges at US hospitals. Patients: A total of 370 patients with barbed suture use were matched with 370 patients with traditional suture use. Intervention: Robotic-assistance hysterectomy. Measurements and Main Results: A total of 5443 hospital inpatient discharges were identified as having a robotic-assistance hysterectomy during study timeframe. Traditional suture was used in 2673 (50%) and barbed suture was used in 370 (7%) of those discharges. Discharges where LapraTy was used (5%) or when no suture use was reported (39%) were excluded from the analysis. The adjusted mean OR time was approximately 24 minutes shorter when barbed suture was used (p<.001). The mean anesthesia time was 25 minutes shorter when Conclusion: Use of a barbed suture is associated with a shorter OR and anesthesia time in patients with robotic-assistance hysterectomies, and an overall reduction in surgery costs.

title: Elevated Serum and Peritoneal Interleukin-33 Levels in Deeply Infiltrating Endometriosis
authors_raw: P. Santulli, B. Borghese, S. Chouzenoux, I. Streuli, ... C. Chapron
title_normalized: elevated serum and peritoneal interleukin 33 levels in deeply infiltrating endometriosis
abstract_text: Study Objective: Interleukin 33 (IL-33) is a cytokine involved in fibrotic disorders. We have analyzed IL-33 levels in sera and peritoneal fluids of women with various forms of endometriosis and investigated the correlation with disease activity. Design: Prospective laboratory study between January 2005 and December 2010. Setting: University tertiary referral center. Patients: Five hundred and ten women with histologically proven endometriosis and 132 endometriosis-free controls have been enrolled in this study. Intervention: Complete surgical exploration of the abdominopelvic cavity was performed in each patient with a radical exeresis of endometriotic lesions. IL-33 was measured by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay in sera and peritoneal fluids, and the concentrations correlated with the extent and the severity of endometriotic lesions. Peritoneal IL-33 was higher in DIE than in endometriosis-free women (median, 642.0 pg/ml; range, 25.9 to 3350.6 versus median, 194.2 pg/ml; range, 12.7 to 1818.2, respectively; p = 0.003). We found Conclusion: IL-33 is abnormally elevated in women with endometriosis and principally in DIE. Elevated serum IL-33 is correlated with intensity of preoperative painful symptoms, and with the extent and severity of the DIE. Patients: Two-hundred and forty-four consecutive patients with histologically proven low rectal endometriosis. Intervention: Complete surgical exeresis of endometriotic lesions. 74 women with (IC+) and 170 without (IC-) ileocecal involvement were compared using medical history, surgery, disease, and pathology. Measurements and Main Results: 30% of women with low rectal endometriosis have associated ileocecal involvement. As compared to the IC- group (n = 170), women in group IC+ (n = 74) were older (34.2 vs 32.5; p <0.05), had a longer disease duration (month) (63.3 vs 52.2; p = 0.05), a higher number of previous surgical procedure (p <0.05), a more severe rAFS score (p <0.001), an additional sigmoid lesion (p <0.01), and an increased number of deep endometriotic lesions (p <0.05). There were no significant differences between the Conclusion: Ileocecal involvement is associated with increased severity of low rectal endometriosis, as evidenced by a more ancient disease, a more severe extension (number of lesions, rAFS score), and a greater prevalence of sigmoid lesions. authors_raw: B. Borghese, M. Chartier, C. Souza, P. Santulli, ... C. Chapron
title_normalized: abo and rhesus blood groups and risk of endometriosis
abstract_text: Study Objective: To compare ABO and rhesus blood groups of patients with and without endometriosis. Design: Cross-sectional study using prospectively collected data. Setting: University tertiary referral center. Patients: 633 women less than 42 years old operated for benign gynecological indications from February 2005 to January 2011. The study group included 311 patients with histologically proved endometriosis (superficial SUP n = 53; endometrioma OMA n = 110; deep infiltrating endometriosis DIE n = 148). The control group included 322 patients without any visual lesion of endometriosis, as checked at the time of surgery. Intervention: Complete surgical excision of symptomatic endometriotic lesions. Measurements and Main Results: Statistical methods included chi-square test and odds ratios with 95% CI. Distribution of ABO blood groups in women with endometriosis did not differed significantly from that of the women without endometriosis (p = 0,72). On the other hand, the negative rhesus group was more predominant in women with endometriosis (23.2 %), as compared to that of the healthy women (15.2 %) (p < 0,01). The overall risk of women with endometriosis and negative rhesus blood group was 1.68 (95%CI: 1.12 to 2.50). Conclusion: We showed for the first time that women with endometriosis have a 1.7-fold increased risk in the negative rhesus blood group distribution. ABO groups were not different between cases and controls. The role of rhesus groups in the development of endometriosis remains to be determined.

Patients: One hundred thirty-one female patients (ages 8-91 years) with a preoperative diagnosis of adnexal mass were included. Patients with preoperative signs of advanced ovarian cancer were excluded. Intervention: All patients underwent diagnosis with one or more of the following: imaging, tumor markers, videolaparoscopic management with intraoperative assessment, and/or frozen section. Measurements and Main Results: Of the 131 patients evaluated, 44 had tumor markers examined, 127 had imaging performed (pelvic ultrasound, CT, and MRI), and 87 had specimens evaluated with frozen section. One hundred twenty-nine patients were assessed intraoperatively by an experienced gynecologic oncologist. There were 24 (18.3%) borderline/invasive malignancies and 107 (81.7%) benign pathologies. The single most accurate test was intraoperative impression (96.9% accurate), followed by frozen section (95.4% accurate). Conclusion: Frozen section combined with intraoperative impression is the most accurate method of differentiating between malignant and benign adnexal masses at laparoscopy.

authors_raw: K. Hellman, S. Senapati, F. Tu
title_normalized: optical diagnostics of pain targets during laparoscopic surgery
abstract_text: Study Objective: The causes of uterine pain are poorly understood and the development of optical diagnostics provides an opportunity to improve surgical outcomes. One hypothesis is that some conditions manifest with ischemia and others via inflammation with elevated perfusion. Our own preliminary animal models support the concept that different types of pain result in divergent effects on uterine perfusion and oxygenation. The objective of this study is to evaluate the significance of uterine Design: Feasibility study. Setting: NorthShore University HealthSystem. Patients: 3 videos of laparoscopic myomectomy utilizing vasopressin. Intervention: We analyzed spectrographic data obtained from surgical video of vasopressin injection into uteri of women undergoing laparoscopic myomectomy. Measurements and Main Results: To test whether oxygenation could be measured in humans laparoscopically, colormetric video analysis (red/blue pixel intensity) was performed in the uterus and adjacent lining. To examine precision, we examined the variability between adjacent frames. Within consecutive video frames there was never more than1.5% variation in spectrographic measurements. To validate accuracy, we examined the time course of vasopressin mediated oxygenation desaturation estimated from the spectrographic signal. Conclusion: Optical spectrography is feasible and with additional study may prove to be an additional diagnostic tool that could be utilized to differentiate pain of ischemic or inflammatory origin.

abstract_text: Study Objective: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease. IL-10 family interleukins (IL-10, IL-19, IL-20 and IL-22) are involved in inflammatory disorders. We have determined the serum levels of IL-10 family interleukins in women with ovarian endometriosis and investigated the correlation of these levels with disease activity. Patients: 219 women, with (n = 112) and without (n = 107) endometriosis, have been enrolled in this study. Intervention: Complete surgical exploration of the abdominopelvic cavity was performed in each patient with a radical exeresis of endometriotic lesions. Measurements and Main Results: IL-10 and IL-20 were undetectables in patients and controls. We found significant Conclusion: Serum IL-19 and IL-22 are abnormally decreased in women with ovarian endometrioma. IL-10 family interleukins may be involved in the pathogenesis of endometriosis.

abstract_text: Study Objective: Resectoscopic endometrial ablation (REA) was introduced in the 1980s as an alternative to hysterectomy to treat abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) from benign causes. Setting: University-affiliated hospital. Patients: 183 women who failed primary resectoscopic endometrial ablation (PREA) underwent RREA by the senior author (GAV). Intervention: After IRB approval, medical records of women who underwent RREA from Jan. 1, 1993 through Dec. 31, 2007 were reviewed. Patient follow-up was conducted by office visits and telephone interview. All RREA were performed under general anesthesia using 1.5% glycine irrigant solution, a 26 F resectoscope with an 8 mm monopolar loop electrode at 120 W of low voltage (cut) waveform (74.7%), rollerball with high voltage (coag) waveform (22.0%) or both (3.3%). Measurements and Main Results: Corresponding median age (range) for PREA and RREA was 40 years (26-70) and 43 years (29-76), respectively. Indications for RREA included uterine bleeding (52.7%), bleeding and pain (25.8%), pain (18.8%), and other (2.7%). Overall RREA complication rate was 5.5% (n = 10): uterine perforation (1); perforation and fluid absorption (2); fluid absorption (1); bleeding (2); false passage (3); and partial ablation (1). Following RREA, 73.2% of women avoided hysterectomy. Conclusion: Median time to failure of PREA is approximately 3 years. RREA is feasible and safe in experienced hands with low complication rate. RREA obviates hysterectomy in 73.2% of women who fail primary REA.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To investigate if self-efficacy and flow are related to the laparoscopic performance among residents in Obstetrics and Gynecology. Background: The non-technical skill self-efficacy; i.e. ones belief in one's ability to succeed in specific situations, has previously been demonstrated to influence simulated laparoscopy. Individual flow experiencing, i.e. “to move or run smoothly with unbroken continuity when performing a task”, has been reported to facilitate growth of skills over time. Design: Within the framework of a prospective randomized study, twenty-eight residents in Obstetrics and Gynecology were enrolled. At baseline residents performed a laparoscopic tubal occlusion and were assessed for self-efficacy and flow. Nineteen received simulator training to a credential level. Nine residents did not receive any type of training prior to surgery and served as control group. The residents subsequently performed and recorded three laparoscopic tubal occlusions. The third operation Setting: The residents were from 21 different hospitals in Sweden. Recorded operations were done at their respective clinics. Measurements and Main Results: At baseline, the two groups exhibited similar results. Mean flow and self-efficacy score assessed after the third tubal occlusion were negatively correlated with operation time, (r = -0.71, p = 0.02) and (r = -0.76, p = 0.007) respectively in the entire study group. The group receiving simulator training scored significantly higher on self-efficacy scores before (p < 0.008) and after surgery (p < 0.003) as well as on flow scores after surgery (p < 0.003) as compared to the untrained group. Conclusion: Simulator training appears to improve flow and self-efficacy during a subsequent surgical procedure. Flow and self-efficacy also appear to have an impact on the surgical result. Further research is needed to investigate how the surgical environment can enhance flow and self-efficacy.

The preoperative exam with visceral slide had a sensitivity=80%, specificity=95%, PPV=66.7%, and Conclusion: The Office Visceral Slide is a simple and reliable test for detecting obliterating periumbilical adhesions in an outpatient setting. abstract_text: Study Objective: To evaluate with histologic analysis of the excised cyst wall and with ultrasonographic follow-up of operated ovaries if excisional surgery for recurrent endometriomas is more harmful to the ovarian reserve than first surgery. Design: Prospective controlled study (Canadian Task Force Classification: II-1). Setting: Tertiary-care university hospitals. Patients: Consecutive patients of reproductive age with pelvic pain and/or infertility undergoing laparoscopic excision of a monolateral ovarian endometrioma larger than 3 cm for a recurrency after previous surgical treatment (11 patients) or operated for the first time (17 patients). Intervention: Laparoscopic excision of the ovarian endometrioma by the stripping technique with a thorough histological analysis of the cyst wall, followed by postoperative sonographic evaluation at 3 months for the measurements of ovarian volumes and antral follicle count (AFC) of both the operated and contralateral nonoperated ovary. Measurements and Main Results: Histological analysis confirmed the endometriotic nature of all excised cysts. The cyst wall specimen was significantly thicker in the recurrent endometrioma group than in the first surgery group (1.7±0.3 mm versus 1.1±0.3 mm, p<0.001). Both main components of the cyst specimen, i.e. endometriosis tissue and ovarian tissue, were more represented in the recurrent endometrioma cyst wall group than in the first surgery group. At sonographic follow-up, the operated ovary had smaller ovarian volumes Conclusion: If compared with endometriomas operated for the first time, surgery for recurrent endometriomas is associated with histological evidence of higher loss of ovarian tissue, and is more harmful to the ovarian reserve as evaluated by ovarian volumes and AFC. Indication to surgery for recurrent endometriomas should therefore be reconsidered with caution.

Design: We collected data on advanced laparoscopic surgery for excision of endometriosis between 2011-2012. Complete data for 74 cases was included. Setting: University Hospital Wales, a large teaching hospital with a specialist endometriosis centre performing advanced laparoscopic surgery. Intervention: All cases underwent a pre-operative prediction of duration of surgery by either the operating surgeon or another minimal access surgeon/resident. Measurements and Main Results: When the surgeon performing the surgery predicted the duration, there was a strong linear correlation with a Pearson R-coefficient of 0.67. When another surgeon, not performing the surgery, predicted the duration there was a weak correlation with a Pearson R-coefficient of 0.32. Regression modelling for the operating surgeons’ was highly accurate, with a regression model gradient of 0.96. For non-operating surgeons, there was a large underestimation of required theatre time with a regression Conclusion: The operating surgeon can accurately predict the duration of surgery when compared to another surgeon/resident, so achieving maximum theatre utilisation. Regression modelling is more accurate than ‘guestimating’ theatre time. This mathematical model can maximise theatre efficiency, reduce financial cost and can be easily implemented.

title: A Novel Program to Reduce the Total Abdominal Hysterectomy Rate in a Community Hospital System to Less Than 10%
authors_raw: JP Lenihan, M. Brower

abstract_text: Study Objective: The purpose of this study was to determine if a novel program emphasizing minimally invasive hysterectomies could influence surgeons to lower the incidence of Total Abdominal Hysterectomies (TAH) to <10%. Design: Prospective cohort analysis of all patients undergoing hysterectomy as to method (Canadian Task Force Classification III). Primary outcome is the reduction in the rate of abdominal hysterectomy to < 10%. Setting: Three hospital Community Healthcare System in the Pacific Northwest. Patients: All women (2969) undergoing a hysterectomy in 2004 and 2007 through 2012. Intervention: A formal program was initiated in 2007 to lower the TAH rate system wide to less than 10% at all hospitals. We encouraged vaginal, laparoscopic and robotic assisted approaches to accomplish this goal. We retrospectively analyzed our rates of hysterectomies by type prior to this. The program had three components: quarterly feedback to the surgeons about their rates of hysterectomy by type compared to their peers, education and training regarding how to avoid open surgery, and encouragement to Measurements and Main Results: The TAH rate was reduced at our main hospitals, TGH/AH, from 45% in 2004 to 9% by the end of 2010; and at a third newly acquired hospital, GSH, from 56% in 2008 to 14% by the end of 2012. This was accomplished by increasing the rate of vaginal (VH) and robotic hysterectomies (RALH). The rate of laparoscopic hysterectomies (LH) decreased at all hospitals after the implementation of robotics. Conclusion: This novel program involving education and training, timely feedback, comparison to peers and encouragement to refer clearly had a significant impact on lowering the TAH rate at all MultiCare hospitals. This program can easily be replicated at other institutions interested in providing more minimally invasive options to their patients.

abstract_text: Study Objective: Minimally invasive approaches to hysterectomy have several advantages to abdominal hysterectomy. Nonetheless, there have been disparities reported in the application of new minimally invasive technologies. The intent of this study was to assess whether racial and socioeconomic factors correlate with hysterectomy approach. Design: Retrospective cohort study. Setting: The Nationwide Inpatient Sample database from the Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project, 2010. Patients: Abdominal, laparoscopic and vaginal hysterectomies performed for menorrhagia or uterine leiomyomata during the year 2010 were identified using the Nationwide Inpatient Sample database. Intervention: Socioeconomic variables including race, median household income quartile and insurance status were analyzed using chi-square and logistic regression to identify independent predictive factors for undergoing minimally invasive hysterectomy. Measurements and Main Results: Of a total of 37,344 hysterectomies for menorrhagia or uterine leiomyomata identified, 24,435 were performed via an abdominal approach while 4,587 were performed vaginally and 8,322 laparoscopically. In comparison to abdominal hysterectomies, laparoscopic hysterectomies were more commonly performed on Caucasian than African American women (OR 2.5, P < .0001) in higher income areas (OR 1.3, P < .0001) with private insurance (OR 1.3, P < .0001). These disparities were even more profound for the Conclusion: The adoption of newer minimally invasive technologies for benign hysterectomy has shown racial and socioeconomic discrepancies. However, even access to vaginal hysterectomy remains significantly dependent on race and insurance status.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To compare annual trends of perioperative resource utilization and morbidity for benign hysterectomy by surgical approach using a national patient cohort from the American College of Surgeons National Surgical Quality Improvement Program (NSQIP). Due to increasing surgeon experience, we hypothesize that patients will experience less morbidity and surgeries will use less resources over time. Design: Retrospective analysis of total abdominal (TAH), total laparoscopic (TLH), laparoscopic supracervical (LSCH), laparoscopic-assisted vaginal (LAVH), and total vaginal hysterectomies (TVH), with or without bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO) performed for benign disease. Patients were followed 30 days postoperatively. Setting: A nationally representative patient cohort in the United States from January 2008 to December 2011. Patients: 25457 women (ages 18-90 years old) at participating NSQIP hospitals. Intervention: TAH, TLH, LSCH, LAVH, and TVH, with or without BSO. Measurements and Main Results: Over the study period TAH and TVH decreased from 43% and 22% of total cases in 2008 to 36% and 14% of total cases in 2011. TLH increased from 5% to 21%, and LSCH and LAVH were stable at about 14-20% of cases each during the study period. The most common indications for hysterectomy, starting with most common, were uterine leiomyomas, menorrhagia, pelvic organ prolapse, dysmenorrhea, and ovarian cysts. Over the study period, there was no statistically significant change in operative time (OT), Conclusion: Using the measures of operative time, hospital length of stay, and need for repeat surgery, there is no measurable decrease in resource utilization over the recent study period for any particular approach to hysterectomy. Developing patient-centered quality improvement metrics is an important need for minimally invasive surgeons.

Intervention: Laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy with or without a bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Measurements and Main Results: Of the 383 laparoscopic hysterectomies performed, 256 had post-operative follow-up. Post-operative follow-up was low (67%) as the remaining patients were seen by their referring physician. Group A included 187 patients (73%) and Group B included 69 patients (27%). Group B was separated into two groups; Group B1 included patients with bleeding that resolved within 12 weeks post-operatively (n=40), and Group B2 included patients with bleeding starting or persisting greater than 12 weeks Conclusion: Adenomyosis was not found to be associated with post-operative bleeding after a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy. In addition, secondary outcomes such as operative time (p=0.79), ΔHb (p=0.41), postoperative hospital stay (p=0.16), postoperative return to normal activity (p=0.11) and complication rate also did not show any significant Conclusion: The addition of bilateral salpingectomy to TLH for prevention of ovarian cancer in women who do not carry a BRCA1/2 mutations do not show negative effects on the ovarian function. abstract_text: Study Objective: A potential limitation of robotic staging in endometrial cancer is the limited capacity to reach the para-aortic nodes to the level of the renal vessels. Our goal was to evaluate the feasibility and efficacy of extra-peritoneal (EP) laparoscopic para-aortic lymphadenectomy compared to trans-peritoneal (TP) lymphadenectomy by laparoscopy or robotic surgery in endometrial cancer staging. Design: IRB approval was obtained for this retrospective analysis of 197 patients who underwent hysterectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy for endometrial cancer. Three groups were compared: Group 1 included EP laparoscopic para-aortic lymphadenectomy and a robotic hysterectomy and pelvic lymphadenectomy (N=35); Group 2 included laparoscopic hysterectomy and TP pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy (N=108); and Group 3 included robotic hysterectomy and TP pelvic and para-aortic Setting: Southwestern tertiary academic institution. Patients: One hundred ninety-seven women with endometrial cancer. Intervention: Extra-peritoneal para-aortic lymphadenectomy or trans-peritoneal para-aortic lymphadenectomy. Measurements and Main Results: Median age was similar across all groups (62 v 61 v 62y from Groups 1, 2, and 3 respectively; p=0.77). A higher median body mass index (BMI) was noted in Group 1 compared with Group 2 and 3 (34.8 v 28.4 v 30.2 kg/m2; p=0.03). Additionally, a significantly higher median total number of para-aortic lymph nodes were obtained in Group1 compared to both Group 2 and 3 (median 10 v 5 v 4 nodes respectively; p <0.001), but with no significant difference in nodal count between Group 2 and 3 (p=0.64). Conclusion: Extra-peritoneal laparoscopic para-aortic lymphadenectomy was noted to result in a higher number of para-aortic lymph nodes removed compared to both laparoscopic and robotic trans-peritoneal lymphadenectomy in our cohort. This alternate approach should be considered when performing endometrial cancer staging, particularly in obese women.

Intervention: Laparoscopic or abdominal nonconservative hysterectomy with pelvic and paraaortic lymph node dissection when indicated. Measurements and Main Results: Multivariate analyses were used to compare the patients with and without MRI misdiagnosis for myometrial and cervical invasion. Intervention: • Morphologic examination: Cells were treated with simvastatin at various concentrations and morphologic appearance was examined using a light microscope.MTT Cell Proliferation Assay result for cells treated with simvastatin at serial concentrations from 0.1 μM to 30 μM with incubation end points at 24, 48, and 72 hours. A: Dose-Response Curve. B: Time-Response Curve. • Apoptosis study: HuLM were treated with simvastatin at 1 μM and 30 μM for 24 and 48 hours before assessing apoptosis. Cells Measurements and Main Results: Our results demonstrate that: • Simvastatin induces apoptosis of human leiomyoma cells in a dose and time-dependent fashion. • Simvastatin inhibits cellular proliferation in a dose and time-dependent fashion. Conclusion: Simvastatin treatment of huLM cells induces apoptosis, inhibits proliferation, induces morphologic changes and modulates intracellular signaling pathways. abstract_text: Study Objective: To compare surgical outcomes between patients affected by benign gynaecological diseases submitted to total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH) performed using the “Five Trocar Method” and women in which standard technique was performed. Design: Retrospective case-control study. Setting: Department of Obstetrics and Gynaecology, Magna Graecia University of Catanzaro, Italy. Patients: Twenty-five women treated by the 5-port laparoscopic access (experimental group) and 42 by the traditional 4-port access (controlgroup) for benign diseases between January 2009 and December 2012. Intervention: We compared data of selected overweight and obese women submitted to 5-port laparoscopic access or conventional 4-port approach performing TLH. Measurements and Main Results: No significant difference in terms of clinical and anthropometric characteristics between the two groups was detected. Only one case of early laparotomic conversion (1.49%), specifically in a patient who underwent conventional approach, was observed. No significant difference in anesthesiological and postoperative complications was observed between groups. The mean operative time was statistically longer in the conventional laparoscopic group than in the 5-port laparoscopic access group Conclusion: The 5-port laparoscopic approach provides optimal exposure of the operative field but it also helps the execution of different surgical steps during TLH to increase the efficiency and confort of the procedure, specifically in obese women. title: Laser in Gynecology: Correction of Age-Related Changes of Vulva and Vaginal Mucosa
authors_raw: E.V. Piskunova
title_normalized: laser in gynecology correction of age related changes of vulva and vaginal mucosa
abstract_text: Study Objective: Assess the clinical effect of laser therapy in the correction of ageing vulva and vaginal mucosa; assess the feasibility of using laser treatment algorithm. Design: Retrospective study of 90 patients, between February 2012 and January 2013. Setting: Gynecology Department, Cosmetology and Plastic Surgery Center, Yekaterinburg. Patients: Group 1: Women aged 47 to 65 years (n= 50) with symptoms of degenerative changes of the vulva and vaginal mucosa of different severity associated with peri- and postmenopausal changes. Group 2 (controls, n= 40): women of same age with similar clinical manifestations. The patients of both groups matched in somatic status and obstetric history and presented with similar complaints of dryness, itching or burning of the vulva, dyspareunia, and thus depression and impaired social adjustment. Intervention: Patients of both groups were assessed by the standard protocol. All patients underwent biopsy with subsequent histological examination. Therapy was prescribed under the protocol including hormone replacement therapy, local treatment, desensitizers and vitamins. In addition to the protocol, Group 1 patients received 1 to 3 CO2 laser treatments with a month interval for vulvovaginal rejuvenation using Smart Xide 2 Gyn (Italy). Measurements and Main Results: In Group 1 at 6 months of treatment positive effect was noted in 100% of cases, 86% were fully satisfied with the result. Some improvement including histological findings was noted after the 1st treatment in 52% of patients. In controls at 6 months positive effect was noted in 62% of patients, only 18% were fully satisfied and 20% had no positive effect. Conclusion: The use of laser therapy in the treatment algorithm of women with degenerative changes of the vulva and vaginal mucosa is expedient, pathogenetically justified and gives pronounced clinical effect. Laser therapy launches collagen synthesis, provides cellular rejuvenation and significantly improves tissues, which gives a pronounced therapeutic and aesthetic effect and thus improves the quality of life.

title: Rectovaginal Fistula Repair Techniques
authors_raw: P.A. Nosti, A.I. Sokol

abstract_text: Objective: The purpose of this video is to demonstrate two techniques for transperineal rectovaginal fistula (RVF) repair. We will also illustrate the use of a Martius flap to reinforce an RVF repair in a patient with Crohn's disease. Description: The surgical approach to the repair of RVF is dependent upon the location and size of the fistula as well as the tissue quality. The key steps of RVF repair include wide mobilization of tissue planes, complete excision of fistulous track, multilayer tension-free closure and when applicable, sphincter repair. Conclusion: To successfully repair RVFs, the surgeon must identify the size and location of the fistula tract and its association to the sphincter. Inclusion of a Martius flap may improve outcomes in patients with poor tissue quality. In all cases, the surgeon must achieve a multilayer tension-free closure.

abstract_text: Study Objective: Distractions such as answering telephone calls and pagers by the surgical team are common occurrences in the operating room. The secondary objective was to evaluate accuracy of responses to pager distraction questions and record the number of unsafe clinical decisions. Design: Randomized cross-over study. Setting: Tertiary teaching hospital at McMaster University, Hamilton, Ontario, Canada. Patients: Gynecology residents from all levels of training who volunteered to perform a laparoscopic task on a virtual reality simulator. Intervention: Participants performed the salpingectomy module under both distracted and quiet situations; but where the sequence was randomly assigned. The distracted phase consisted of a pager ringing followed by the subject being asked clinical questions based on generic ward patients. Measurements and Main Results: 30 residents participated. There was no difference between distracted and quiet situations in paired analysis for mean time (seconds) to task completion [426 (SD133) vs 440 (SD186)] and mean operative blood loss (mL) [73.14 (SD106) vs 112.70 (SD358)]. Subjects were more likely to successfully complete the surgical task in the allotted time under the quiet compared to distraction situation (OR=11.3, 95% CI 1.14-110.4, p=0.03). The mean proportion of correct responses to clinical questions was Conclusion: Pager distractions hindered the ability to complete the task in the allotted time, without affecting other variables of surgical performance. Distractions may have a profound impact on patient safety on the wards as unsafe clinical decisions are frequently made while operating.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To evaluate the impact of external stressors on laparoscopic skills of Obstetrics and Gynecology Residents. Design: Observational Prospective Cohort Study. Setting: Urban teaching hospital. Patients: 19 Obstetrics and Gynecology Residents, Post Graduate Years 1-4. Intervention: Residents engaged in novel laparoscopic tasks during two sessions six months apart, a baseline session and then an intervention session. Each session consisted of four simulated laparoscopic training modules, which tested dissection skills, bimanual dexterity with depth perception, hand-to-hand coordination, and hand-eye coordination with bi-manual dexterity. The first session evaluated resident performance, without external stressors, providing baseline timing and accuracy data. The second Measurements and Main Results: Performance was assessed by two outcome measures: total time required to successfully complete each module (speed) and number of penalties accrued during module completion (accuracy). Subjective measure of resident stress was evaluated two weeks prior and then immediately following the second session using the validated Perceived Stress Survey (PSS). Data was analyzed using paired t tests and Wilcoxon signed-rank test with a p value of less than 0.05 considered as significant. The study Conclusion: Stress appears to impact laparoscopic performance by decreasing total time for task completion, but increasing errors in novel laparoscopic tasks. Our data indicate that stress may negatively affect accuracy of surgical tasks. Given the critical need for accuracy in laparoscopic surgery, the data support the need to minimize stress in the learning environment for trainees approaching novel surgical tasks.

Task1 used a Maryland to move peas from a dish into a hole. Task2 involved placing letters onto a checkerboard using Marylands. Task3 and Task4 were the peg transfer and intracorporeal suturing from the FLS program. All tasks were Measurements and Main Results: All participants completed all portions of the protocol. Compared to novices, experts perfomed significantly better than novices on all 4 tasks; Task1 (47 vs. 74, P<0.05), Task2 (112 vs 190, P<0.05), Task3 (95 vs 157, P<0.05). Task4 (238 vs 422, P<0.05). authors_raw: M.M. Hanafi

abstract_text: Study Objective: Evaluate the accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), and negative predictive value (NPV) of the diagnosis of Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, and combined, by the use of transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) in comparison to the histopathological findings. Design: Retrospective study of 163 patients with a preoperative transvaginal ultrasound diagnosis of adenomyosis, leiomyoma and combined. Setting: Private Practice Atlanta Georgia. Patients: 163 patients with a preoperative transvaginal ultrasound diagnosis of adenomyosis, leiomyoma and combined. Of 163 patients. Intervention: 130 patients underwent hysterectomy. 33 symptomatic patients diagnosed with adenomyosis and leiomyoma via TVS underwent myomectomy with excision of the surrounding myometrium which contained possible adenomyosis. Following surgery, a histopathological examination was performed by the hospital pathologists. Measurements and Main Results: Histopathological confirmation of the TVS diagnosis of adenomyosis (n=123) was positive in 93 patients (75.61%) and negative in 30 patients (24.39%). The sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy of TVS in the diagnosis of adenomyosis was 84.55% (p<.0001), 43.40% (p=0.41), 71.17% respectively. TVS diagnosis of adenomyosis is sensitive but not specific. Histopathological confirmation of the TVS diagnosis of leiomyoma (n=134) was positive in 133 patients (99.25%) and negative in 1 patient (0.75%). Conclusion: Transvaginal ultrasound is a valuable non-invasive method that should be utilized in the diagnosis of Leiomyomata and combined Adenomyosis and Leiomyoma. TVS is sensitive but is not specific in the diagnosis of Adenomyosis only.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To estimate, after correction for patient's factors, to what extent blood loss, operative time, and adverse events are decisive factors for the successful outcome of laparoscopic hysterectomy (LH). Secondly, to what extent a successful outcome can be predicted from surgical experience or other measures of surgical skill? Design: A nationwide multivariate 1-year cohort analysis with gynecologists who perform LH. Intervention: The primary outcomes were blood loss, operative time, and adverse events. The procedures were corrected for multiple covariates in a mixed-effects logistic regression model. Furthermore, all primary outcomes were related to experience and the influence of individual surgical skills factors. Cumulative summation statistics was applied in order to direct derailing individual performance. Measurements and Main Results: 1.534 LH's were analyzed for 79 surgeons. The success of the surgical outcome was significantly influenced by uterus weight, body mass index, previous abdominal surgeries and the type of LH. Surgical experience also predicted the successful outcome of laparoscopic hysterectomy with respect to blood loss and adverse events (P=.048 and .036, respectively). A significant improvement in surgical outcomes tends to continue up to approximately 125 procedures. Independently from surgical experience, Conclusion: After adjusting for risk factors, it was shown that an increase in experience positively predicted successful outcome in LH with respect to blood loss and adverse events. However, the independent surgical skills factor shows a large variation in proficiency between individuals. The fact that a surgeon has performed many LH's does not necessarily guarantee good surgical outcome. Continuously proficiency curve monitoring, by means of cumulative summation analysis can flag in time derailing

Setting: A single Ob-Gyn residency training program located in an academic medical center in the southeastern US. Patients: All residents in the program (PGY 1-4, n=15). Intervention: The FLS curriculum, a two-part program consisting of five online modules related to cognitive knowledge in laparoscopic surgery and technical training in five manual skills (e.g., ligating loop). Residents practiced to attain time and accuracy standards for each task per protocol. Measurements and Main Results: To test the efficacy of the FLS program as a curricular initiative, residents completed a pre- and post-test on the manual skills training and a post-test only on the cognitive knowledge modules. All of the residents completed the manual skills training, while only 27% of the residents completed the online cognitive knowledge modules. The difference between pre- (M=138.02, SD=73.50) and post-test (M=453.13, SD=29.14) manual skills scores was statistically significant (t(14)=-17.34, p=.000), Conclusion: The results of this study suggest that the FLS Program holds promise as a curricular initiative for developing laparoscopic skills and knowledge in Ob-Gyn residents. abstract_text: Study Objective: The elaboration of non-invasive method to diagnose endometriosis is one of the hot topics in endometriosis research. This is the first study assessing CA125 levels in a large cohort of women according to the surgical and histological classification of endometriotic lesions in SUP (Superficial), OMA (Endometrioma) and DIE (Deeply infiltrating endometriosis) and in surgically investigated controls without Design: A prospective laboratory study. Conclusion: Our study results show, for the first time, a significant increase in CA125 serum levels in women with OMA and/or DIE. Patients were divided into two groups according to ureteral histological patterns (Group 1: stromal nodule; Group 2: fibrotic nodule) and clinical and surgical findings were compared. Out of 1500 patients, 413 had deep infiltrating endometriosis. In those women ureteral involvement was present in 184 patients. Conclusion: The ureteral endometriosis is very frequent since 12,43% are affected. The ureter represents a ‘target’ of endometriosis and not a secondary localization of an inflammatory pelvic state. Hystological type of the ureteral nodule don't correlate with the degree of wall infiltration. From our results we deduced that ureteral endometriosis is in not an infiltrating disease in the majority of cases so a conservative management is justified.

title: Endometriosis Obliterating the Pouch of Douglas - Was It Ever Rectovaginal?
authors_raw: O. Elsaccar, D.C. Martin, L. Detti, V.M. Feldbaum, A. Azari
title_normalized: endometriosis obliterating the pouch of douglas was it ever rectovaginal
abstract_text: Study Objective: In 1914, Thomas Cullen used the term the term “rectovaginal septum” to describe adenomyomas (currently called endometriosis) situated in the area between the cervix and the rectum. His and others’ illustrations are still used to classify rectovaginal septum involvement from endometriosis. However, his illustrations are in the area of the Pouch of Douglas and do notextend to the rectovaginal septum Kuhn (1982) noted that the rectovaginal pouch extends caudally to the level of the middle third of Design: Eighteen illustrations of rectovaginal endometriosis were evaluated from 5 publications between 1903 and 1922. Setting: Literature review. Patients: Eighteent patients in the literature. Intervention: Historical surgery. Measurements and Main Results: In reviewing the pathology drawings, in each figure we identified the degree of rectal, vaginal and cervical involvement seen. All eighteen had retrocervical and rectal involvement. Thirteen involved the vagina and seven penetrated the vagina. No drawings showed involvement of the normal anatomic area of the rectovaginal septum. Conclusion: Classical publications from 1903 to 1922 demonstrated endometriosis obliterating the Pouch of Douglas were in the area of the Pouch of Douglas and not in the rectovaginal septum. This type endometriosis is a retrocervical and rectal disease with involvement of the vagina in most cases. No extension to the level of the rectovaginal septum was noted. We advocate revisiting the classical definition to improve and update communication among current investigators.

abstract_text: Study Objective: The goal of this study was to compare the safety and efficiency of different surgical treatments employed on patients presenting with deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) with lower intestinal involvement. Design: This is a prospective study conducted on clinical records. No follow-up was deemed necessary at the moment. Setting: University Hospitals of Strasbourg. All surgeries were performed by the same operator. Patients: 176 consecutive patients presenting with DIE with lower intestinal involvement, who underwent surgery between 2005 and 2011. In all cases, histological confirmation of the endometriosis was obtained. Intervention: The study considered results of four different surgical procedures: nodule shaving, mucosal skinning, discoid resection and segmental resection. Nodule shaving was defined as a surgical act that excised affected tissue from the intestinal wall but did not reach the mucosal layer. Mucosal skinning was defined as a surgical act that excised affected tissue from the intestinal wall that reached but did not breach the mucosal layer. Discoid resections were performed by excising the affected tissue Measurements and Main Results: A comparison was made between shaving, mucosal skinning, discoid resection (G1, “conservative” treatments, 133 patients) and segmental resection (G2, “demolitive” treatment, 43 patients). Safety was evaluated by comparing complication rates, both intra and postoperative. The study showed a significative difference in overall complication rates (16% vs 37%). Efficiency was assessed by comparing average operative time and length of hospital stay. The study showed a statistically significant Conclusion: The study, albeit in the presence of non trivial selection biases, shows how conservative treatments remain safer and more efficient than demolitive treatments, even in the hands of an experienced surgeon. Additional randomized prospective studies should be performed to compare the two groups without any selection bias, as well as to evaluate the effectiveness of each approach on pain reduction and recurrence rates.

title: Comparison of Three Dimensional Ultrasound Features of Uterine Junctional Zone in Patients with Ovarian or Deep Infiltrating Endometriosis

abstract_text: Study Objective: To assess by three-dimensional (3D) transvaginal sonography (TVS) the detectable alterations of the uterine junctional zone (JZ) in patients with endometriomas only (OMAs) or with isolated deep endometriosis (DIE) and to compare these findings to those without pelvic endometriosis. Design: Prospective analysis of JZ 3D TVS features in secretive phase of the cycle in patients with TVS signs of pelvic endometriosis, never treated surgically. Setting: University hospitals. Patients: 80 patients (ages 23-35yrs) with OMAs or DIE at TVS. Patients with both OMAs and DIE or >35yrs or with previous pelvic surgery were excluded. A control group of 20 patients without endometriosis at TVS and confirmed by laparoscopy and histology was assessed. Intervention: Uterine multiplanar sections obtained by 3D TVS to evaluate JZ features. Measurements and Main Results: 38 patients had only OMAs and 42 only DIE at TVS. The maximum thickness of JZ (JZ max) in patients with DIE was significantly greater than in patients with OMAs and those without endometriosis (6.53±2.19mm vs 4.19±0.77mm vs 4.80±1.00mm). The features of JZ appeared similar in patients with OMAs and without endometriosis, whereas they are statistically different if correlated to patients DIE. Conclusion: JZ thickness and its alterations are different in patients with DIE compared to those with OMAs and without endometriosis. Since these JZ ultrasound features are mostly associated with adenomyosis, a correlation between DIE and JZ hyperplasia and adenomyosis could be hypothesized.

title: Laparoscopic Hysterectomy for Cervical Fibroids – Strategies and Techniques
authors_raw: A.M. Ecker, D. Lum, T.T.M. Lee
title_normalized: laparoscopic hysterectomy for cervical fibroids strategies and techniques
abstract_text: Background: Cervical fibroids are rare, comprising only 2% of uterine fibroids. They can cause labor dystocia in pregnancy as well as dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia and pressure symptoms with proximity to the bladder, urethra or rectum. Surgical options include myomectomy or hysterectomy. Here we present a case of definitive management with a total laparoscopic hysterectomy. Case: 43yo gravida 2, para 2 woman, who presented with menometrorrhagia and pelvic pressure. On exam, she had an enlarged fibroid uterus with a 7cm fibroid replacing most of the cervix, causing it to be dilated to 2cm with 90% effacement. She desired definitive management with hysterectomy. Conclusion: Here we present strategies to surgically approach a cervical fibroid at the time of hysterectomy. These strategies include placement of ureteral stents, ligation of the superior vaginal artery and of the uterine artery at its origin, ureterolysis and placement of a vaginal retractor to aid in successfully completing the hysterectomy.

abstract_text: Background: Our aim is to describe the application of the latest technology ultra-fine instrumentation in our total laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure. Methods: For this method we use two 3mm and two 2mm ports, with two ultra-fine Endo Relief forceps. The two millimeter Endo Relief instruments are only available in Japan. We have applied this approach to 5 cases with comparatively small sized uterine pathology. Results: As the skin and peritoneal incisions were quite small, patient recovery was quick. The skin incisions on healing were almost invisible. Discussion: Although other minimally invasive techniques exist which offer near scar less results, they may require larger incisions and often need specialized experience to perform. This surgery offers the least invasive and most cosmetically appealing result and can be performed in the same way as standard multiport surgery. Reduced port size mini–laparoscopy is one way to reduce patient trauma while using preexisting techniques.

There was no difference in the mean age of women with unexpected sarcoma compared to those with benign pathology; however, women with Conclusion: Unexpected uterine and cervical cancers, found in 1.6% of hysterectomies performed for benign indications are important considerations for perioperative planning of minimally invasive surgery. title: Pelvic Spindle Cell Neoplasms Following Laparoscopic Hysterectomy or Myomectomy with Power Morcellation
authors_raw: K.A. Hartzell, J. Tan-Kim, T.A. Harrison
title_normalized: pelvic spindle cell neoplasms following laparoscopic hysterectomy or myomectomy with power morcellation
abstract_text: Study Objective: To describe the incidence, and risk factors for development of spindle cell neoplasms, including parasitic leiomyomas and uterine sarcoma, at the time of laparoscopic hysterectomy or myomectomy with power morcellation. Design: Retrospective chart review. Setting: Kaiser Permanente Medical Center, San Diego. Patients: 3523 women who underwent laparoscopic hysterectomy or myomectomy from 2000 to 2012. Intervention: Univariate analyses were conducted to identify potential risk factors for parasitic leiomyomas and uterine sarcoma. Significant values (p<0.1) and those characteristics that were biologically plausible risk factors were put into a multivariate logistic regression model. Measurements and Main Results: 941 patients underwent power morcellation at the time of laparoscopic hysterectomy or myomectomy. Of these, 10/941 (1.1%) were diagnosed with spindle cell neoplasms. For parasitic leiomyomas (n=4), median age was 35 years (range 32-40), and median time to second presentation was 5 years (range 4-11). On multivariate analysis, younger age (OR 0.7, p=0.001, CI 0.54-0.90) and uterine weight >350g (OR 13, p=0.046, CI 1.05-163.74) were associated with higher risk of developing parasitic leiomyomas. Conclusion: Younger age and uterine weight >350g are risk factors for parasitic leiomyomas after power morcellation, and menopause is a risk factor for uterine sarcoma. Patients should be counseled about these potential complications prior to undergoing power morcellation, and consideration should be given to avoiding morcellation in these populations.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To assess the incidence and outcome of uterine sarcomas diagnosed among a continuous cohort of 2824 patients referred for fibroid treatment in an academic setting with minimally invasive procedures. Design: Prospective study (Canadian Task Force classification II-3). Setting: University Hospital, Tertiary Center. Patients: Referred patients for treatment of fibroids between 01.01.2002 and 12.31.2013. Intervention: All patients had a clinical examination, endometrial sampling, pelvic ultrasound, MRI reviewed by two different radiologists, one being a senior advisor in oncologic radiology. Patients were treated either by Uterine Artery Embolization (UAE), first described in our department in 1997, hysteroscopy, laparoscopy, a combined procedure first described in 2002 (CESAM), vaginal procedure or by laparotomy. The diagnosis of uterine sarcoma was reviewed by a panel of senior pathologists. Measurements and Main Results: 2824 patients were referred for treatment of uterine fibroids during a 12 years period. Patients were from over 20 different ethnical origins. Among them 579 patients were treated by laparoscopy, 263 patients with a vaginal procedure, and 262 patients by UAE. Six patients had a final diagnosis of uterine sarcoma. None of them was treated by a minimally invasive procedure nor had a uterine morcellation. One patient had a ruptured uterine sarcoma and operated in emergency. No hazard due to Conclusion: The incidence of uterine sarcoma in a continuous population of 2824 patients of diverse ethnical origins referred for treatment of fibroids in a tertiary center was 0.21%. All sarcomas had a suspected diagnosis of malignancy or cellular fibroid prior to surgery. No hazard was reported due to the morcellation of an unrecognized sarcoma. A thorough workup prior to surgery including MRI and office hysteroscopy could help in counseling and preventing from accidental morcellation of a malignancy.

Patients: Incident cases of endometrial cancer from April 2002-March 2012 were identified in the provincial cancer registry. Intervention: Record linkages were made with other healthcare databases to determine type of hysterectomy, (laparoscopic or abdominal +/- staging), year of diagnosis, comorbidities, location of residence, surgeon and hospital type. Adjusting for age, comorbidity score, income Conclusion: This population-based cohort study confirms the increased use over time of laparoscopic surgery to treat endometrial cancers in Ontario, and demonstrates the benefits of decreased hospital stay and decreased patient morbidity. Intervention: An evidence-based App was created containing the full text of the guideline, separated in chapters, with links to background information and instructions. Furthermore, the app includes a decision-aid based on the recommendations within the guideline. Measurements and Main Results: For reasons of transparency and validity, a rigorous methodology was applied on defining the decision-aid: recommendations are copied to the letter, links to the full text of the guideline describing the reasoning behind the recommendations are provided and pathways are clarified to the users by visualising the flow charts. The flow charts are subdivided in 6 topics including recognition of symptoms, clinical examination and establishing diagnosis, and treatment of endometriosis-associated pain Conclusion: In conclusion, nowadays, clinicians are oriented towards smartphones and internet, rather than textbooks, in search for information and assistance. Therefore, ESHRE has developed an evidence-based App on endometriosis with the aim of improving implementation of the ESHRE guideline and hence, improving the quality of care and the quality of life of women with endometriosis.

abstract_text: Study Objective: A collection of several validated questionnaires are often used to assess patients with endometriosis. It has been suggested that this is burdensome and not useful for patients. We aimed to establish whether this was indeed the case. Design: A prospective questionnaire based study. We asked patients with surgically confirmed endometriosis to fill out questionnaires together with a QQ10; a validated questionnaire that assesses value and burden to a patient of other questionnaires. One questionnaire, consisting of a shortened EHP30 together with the EQ5D, was used for superficial endometriosis, whilst another consisting of EHP30, EQ5D, GIQLI and 4 visual analogue scales was used for those with severe rectovaginal disease. Setting: Royal Surrey County Hospital NHS Foundation Trust, a tertiary referral centre for endometriosis. Patients: Patients with surgically confirmed endometriosis were asked to take part. Measurements and Main Results: 95 patients filled out the qq10 with 7 patients declining to complete the questionnaire. 60 completed the rectovaginal questionnaire together with the QQ10 whilst 35 completed the shortened questionnaire together with the QQ10. A score above 50% means the questionnaires are considered to be very valuable by the patients while a burden score of below 45% is considered to be of low burden to the patient. The mean value score for filling out the questionnaires was 79.6% while the burden score was Conclusion: Patients find filling out validated questionnaires very valuable with little burden. Endometriosis centres should use validated questionnaires for all their patients not only for governance, quality control and scientific assessment but also because patients find it useful.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To determine wheter propranolol has an inhibitory effect on the angiogenesis of endometriosis in an experimental rat model or not. Design: Experimental animal model study. Setting: Hospital laboratory. Patients: Animals-Twenty-four female Wistar albino rats (200-250 g) were used to create a model for surgical induction of endometriosis. Two rats died during the surgeries. Intervention: The rats were randomly divided into treatment (n = 11) and control groups (n = 10), which were treated with daily intraperitoneal propranolol (10 mg/kg) and saline (3 mL), respectively. Study duration was 8 weeks. Measurements and Main Results: Main outcome measures: The volumes and histopathological findings of the implants, and immunochemistry for vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), metalloproteinase (MMP)-2, and MMP-9 were evaluated. Results: Viable endometriotic implants were created in all animals. In the propranolol-treated group, the mean implant volume significantly decreased after treatment (142.5 vs. 32.1 mm3, respectively; p = 0.001), while the mean implant volume significantly increased in the control group (141.0 vs. 174.2 mm3, respectively; p = 0.009). There were also significant reductions in VEGF immunoreactivity scores and both stroma and epithelium MMP-2 and MMP-9 immunoreactivity scores in the propranolol-treated Conclusion: Propranolol suppresses endometrial tissue by its antiangiogenic activity through inhibitory actions on VEGF, MMP-2, and MMP-9. Therefore, propranolol is a promising candidate drug for effective treatment of patients with endometriosis.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To evaluate the invasive phenotype of endometriosis through the assessment of some specific biomarkers of invasiveness such as periostin (POSTN), epithelial mesenchimal transition factors(EMT) and cell stem markers (Oct-4,CXCR4,SOX2). Design: Experimental study. Setting: Department of Experimental & Clinical Medicine, UMG, Catanzaro. Patients: 20 patients with endometriomas, adenomyosis, pelvic or deep endometriosis, 15 patients affected by low-risk endometrioid endometrial cancer (EEC G1-G2) and 20 voluntary women with benign gynecological diseases which were submitted to surgery. Intervention: Specimens of endometriosis, EEC, and healthy endometrium were obtained at the time of surgery, and then employed to develop a 3D-spheroid-cells-culture model. To identify POSTN, EMT and stem cells markers, RT-PCR and immunocytochemistry assays were performed. The differential expression of these biomarkers among endometriosis, EEC and healthy endometrium, and among the 4 endometriosis-subtypes were evaluated. Serum POSTN levels were measured by ELISA in blood samples of all patients. Measurements and Main Results: POSTN levels were significantly higher in cells cultures and serum of EEC compared with endometriosis and controls, and in endometriosis vs. controls. Samples of ECC and endometriosis had higher expression levels of EMT and stem cells markers compared with that of the controls. The endometriomas expressed less epithelial markers and more mesenchymal markers than those of healthy endometrium. Peritoneal lesions, adenomyosis and deep endometriosis showed more epithelial markers expression than Conclusion: POSTN levels are increased in patients with endometriosis and they are related to the EMT and stem cell events, which are essential for the carcinogenesis. Since in patients with endometriomas was shown a direct relationship between mesenchimal factors expression and POSTN levels, this protein detectable in serum, may represent a new promising prognostic biomarker for the assessment of oncologic risk in patients with endometriomas.
