Patients: Total of 102 (34 cases and 68 controls) were included. Intervention: Surgical treatment for endometriosis. Measurements and Main Results: In 14 years, 2539 women had surgery for endometriosis with only 34 (1.34 %) having AWE. The mean age was 35.2 ±5.9 years and median parity was 2 (range 0-5). Clinical exam diagnosed AWE in 41% of cases with C-section scar being the most common site (59%) There was strong correlation between the size on exam or imaging compared to pathology (r2=0.74, P<.001). When compared to controls, cases had significantly higher parity, higher body mass index, more cyclic abdominal pain, less dysmenorrhea, Conclusion: AWE is a rare but unique subtype of endometriosis. Careful history and clinical exam can provide accurate diagnosis and avoid unnecessary delay before surgical intervention. Design: All patients underwent total laparoscopic hysterectomy with ureterolysis for benign and complex disease from September 1, 2011 to September 30, 2013. Setting: Community Hospital. Patients: Total laparoscopic hysterectomy with ureterolysis was performed in 126 consecutive cases. Intervention: A posterior parametrial approach was used to easily identify the surgical landmarks of the pararectal space. Blunt dissection is performed until the desired distal ureter is identified. Intraoperative cystoscopy with indigo carmine was performed to evaluate for the presence or absence of extravasation or ureteral injury. Measurements and Main Results: One cystotomy and one rectotomy was discovered intraoperatively and repaired during surgery. There were no ureteral injuries and all patients with the exception of the rectotomy were discharged home on postoperative day 1. abstract_text: Study Objective: To analyze the clinical success of radiofrequency volumetric thermal ablation (RFVTA) at three years follow up in terms of subject responses to validated questionnaires and surgical re-intervention for myomas. Design: Prospective follow up of patients through 36 months posttreatment in a multicenter, international trial of outpatient, laparoscopic ultrasound-guided RFVTA of symptomatic myomas. Setting: University hospitals and private surgery centers. Patients: One hundred thirty-five premenopausal women (mean age, 42.5 ± 4.6 years; mean BMI, 30.5 ± 6.1) with symptomatic myomas and objectively confirmed heavy menstrual bleeding (≥160 to ≤500 mL). Intervention: Laparoscopic, ultrasound-guided RFVTA. Measurements and Main Results: One hundred four participants were followed prospectively through 36 months after fibroid treatment by RFVTA. For 104 evaluable subjects with 36-month data, change in symptom severity from baseline (60.2 ± 18.8) to 36 months was –32.6 (95% CI, -37.5 to -27.8; p < .001). Health-related quality of life also improved from the baseline value of 39.2 ± 19.2 to 36 months by a change of 38.6 (95% CI, 33.3 to 43.9; p < .001). Patient-reported health-related quality-of-life subscale scores showed Conclusion: Radiofrequency volumetric thermal ablation of uterine myomas resulted in sustained relief from myoma symptoms and continued improvement in health-related quality of life through 36 months postablation. The low re-intervention data through 36 months are a positive outcome for patient wellbeing.

title: Safety, Efficacy and Reproductive Outcomes of Hysteroscopic Outpatient Metroplasty to Expand Dysmorphic Uteri (HOME-DU Technique)
authors_raw: A. Di Spiezio Sardo, S. Bettocchi, P. Florio, M. Spinelli, ... C. Nappi
title_normalized: safety efficacy and reproductive outcomes of hysteroscopic outpatient metroplasty to expand dysmorphic uteri home du technique
abstract_text: Study Objective: To evaluate safety and efficacy of a novel outpatient hysteroscopic technique in improving the volume and the morphology of the uterine cavity of dysmorphic uteri and its effects on reproductive outcomes. Design: Prospective observational study (June 2011 to March 2012). Setting: Universitary Teaching Hospital. Patients: Thirty women with dysmorphic uterus ( T-shaped uteri and tubular-shaped/infantilis uteri), as diagnosed by office hysteroscopy and 3D-TVS and a history of infertility, repeated abortions or pre-term delivery. Intervention: Hysteroscopic Outpatient Metroplasty to Expand Dysmorphic Uteri (HOME-DU technique) consists in making 5-6 mm deep incisions on the fibro-muscular constriction rings in the isthmic area of the uterine side walls and on the anterior and posterior walls from the fundus up to the isthmus with a 5 Fr bipolar electrode in the outpatient setting under conscious sedation, using a 5 mm hysteroscope. A gel to prevent intrauterine adhesions (IUAs) is then applied into the uterine cavity through the Measurements and Main Results: The HOME-DU technique was successfully performed in all cases without complications. We found a net increase of the uterine volume, as measured at 3D-TVS (2.1±0.4 vs 1.4±0.2 cm3 P=.00. Also uterine morphology improved in all patients except one. Mild IUAs were detected in the isthmic area only in 5 patients and easily cut with 5 Fr scissors. At a mean follow-up of 15 months, we found a pregnancy rate of 56% with a term delivery rate of 65%.Post-operative reproductive outcomesInclusion criteriaEmpty Cell Conclusion: Our data support: i) the safety and efficacy of this technique in order to improve the volume and shape of tubular/infantilis uteri and T-shaped uteri without the need of general anesthesia and traditional resectoscopic surgery ii) the notion that the improvement of the uterine cavity volume may result in a significant increase of the reproductive outcomes.

title: Triptorelin, Letrozole and Ulipristal Acetate Treatment before Hysteroscopic Resection of Large Myomas: Prospective Comparative Study
authors_raw: N. Bizzarri, V. Ghirardi, V. Remorgida, P.L. Venturini, S. Ferrero
title_normalized: triptorelin letrozole and ulipristal acetate treatment before hysteroscopic resection of large myomas prospective comparative study
abstract_text: Study Objective: To compare the usefulness of preoperative treatment with triptorelin, letrozole and ulipristal acetate before hysteroscopic removal of large uterine submucosal myomas. Design: Single center prospective non-randomized comparative study. Setting: University Hospital. Patients: Consecutive premenopausal patients undergoing hysteroscopic resection of myomas graded as type 0, type 1 or type 2 according to the FIGO classification with diameter between 20 and 35 mm. Exclusion criteria were: associated polyps; associated non-hysteroscopic surgical procedures; > 2 myomas requiring hysteroscopic resection. Intervention: This study enrolled patients who underwent either direct surgery (group S; n = 23) or 3-month preoperative treatment with triptorelin (3.75 mg every 28 days; group T; n = 20), letrozole (2.5 mg/day; group L; n = 11) or ulipristal acetate (5 mg/day; group U; n = 7). All patients underwent hysteroscopic resection of the myomas. Measurements and Main Results: All medical treatments caused a significant decrease in the volume of myomas (group T, p < 0.001; group L, p < 0.001; group U, p = 0.006); however, the percentage decrease in myoma volume was lower in group U than in group T (p = 0.001) and in group L (p = 0.010). The operative time was higher in group S than in group T (p < 0.001) and in group L (p = 0.001); there was no significant difference in the operative time between group S and group U (p = 0.206). Fluid absorption was lower in group T Conclusion: Preoperative treatment with triptorelin and letrozole facilitate hysteroscopic removal of large uterine submucosal myomas.

title: Diagnostic Work-Up for Postmenopausal Bleeding - A Randomized Controlled Trial
authors_raw: L. van Hanegem, M. Breijer, M. Bongers, B.W. Mol, A. Timmermans
title_normalized: diagnostic work up for postmenopausal bleeding a randomized controlled trial
abstract_text: Study Objective: The diagnostic work-up in women with postmenopausal bleeding is focused on ruling out endometrial cancer. However, when, in women with an endometrial thickness >4mm, endometrial biopsy shows a benign result, it is uncertain whether the diagnostic work-up should be continued with a saline infusion sonography (SIS) or hysteroscopy. Design: Women with postmenopausal bleeding, an endometrial thickness >4mm and endometrial biopsy that showed a benign result, were randomized to further invasive diagnostics or to expectant management. The primary outcome of this randomized trial was recurrent bleeding in the first year. Women received instructions to contact the clinic with recurrent bleeding and were contacted after one year. If they reported recurrent bleeding, they received further diagnostic work-up. Setting: From March 2009 to October 2013, we performed a multicenter randomized trial (NTR2130) in 12 university- and teaching hospitals in the Netherlands. Patients: Patients with postmenopausal bleeding with an endometrial thickness of >4mm, in whom endometrial malignancy was excluded, were randomly assigned to subsequent diagnostic work-up, or to expectant management, using a web-based randomization program. Intervention: Women allocated to the intervention-group underwent SIS and hysteroscopy (with polypectomy) in the same session. Woman allocated to expectant management received diagnostic workup only if they reported recurrent bleeding. Measurements and Main Results: A total of 200 women were enrolled, of whom 98 were assigned to hysteroscopy and 102 to expectant management. Median age at randomization was 57 years and the median endometrial thickness was eight mm. In women allocated to SIS and hysteroscopy, 13.3% of the women had recurrent bleeding at one year of follow-up versus 21% among women allocated to expectant management (RR 0.58; 95% CI 0.24-1.4). Conclusion: We found a non-significant reduction in the prevalence of recurrent bleeding in women who received hysteroscopy (and polypectomy) as compared to women who received expectant management.

authors_raw: T. Issat, J. Beta, M.A. Nowicka, T. Maciejewski, A.J. Jakimiuk

abstract_text: Study Objective: To assess the efficacy of Ketoprofen versus intravaginal Misoprostol for the pain relief during outpatient hysteroscopy. Design: A prospective, randomized, single-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trial Canadian Task Force Classification of Study Designs: I. Setting: Tertiary medical center. Patients: One hundred fifty women referred for hysteroscopy between January and October 2013. Intervention: Women were randomized to receive vaginal Misoprostol (400 μg) with 100 ml of 5% intravenous glucose (Misoprostol arm), intravenous Ketoprofen (Ketonal 50mg/1ml) in 100 ml of 5% glucose with intravaginal placebo (Ketoprofen arm) and vaginal placebo tablets with 100ml of 5% intravenous glucose (Placebo arm) before outpatient hysteroscopy. Measurements and Main Results: A visual analogue scale (VAS) was used for one-dimensional pain assessment. Patients were asked to mark a VAS score before, during, 5 minutes and 15 minutes following the procedure. Median VAS scores during and directly after the anaesthesia-free hysteroscopy were significantly lower in the Misoprostol group when compared to women receiving Ketoprofen or placebo (p=0.020 and p=0.006 respectively). There were no statistical differences between three arms in median VAS score assessed 15 minutes Conclusion: When administrated 4 hours before the procedure, 400μg vaginal Misoprostol seems to reduce the pain during, and immediately after hysteroscopy. The effect does not depend on the age of a patients, hormonal status, parity or type of outpatient hysteroscopy (operative or diagnostic).

abstract_text: Study Objective: To compare the degree of lateral thermal injury (LTI) resulting from monopolar energy and CO2 laser at the time of colpotomy during robotic hysterectomy. Design: A prospective, randomized trial between May and November, 2013. Setting: Mayo Clinic, Arizona; Academic. Patients: Patients were randomized to have the colpotomy performed with monopolar energy (coagulation 40 W) or CO2 laser (Omniguide Inc, Cambridge, MA, 14 W). Women over the age of 18 undergoing a hysterectomy for benign condition were included. Intervention: Vaginal tissue from the 12 and 6 o’clock positions from the specimen side were retrieved and cut into 4 x 3 mm sections and then into 5 micrometer sections. LTI was evaluated using: H&E staining, measuring gross extent of LTI from colpotomy edge (mm), Masson’s Trichrome staining, measuring microscopic coagulation (microns) and Masson’s Trichrome staining, measuring extent of tissue edema (microns). Slides were evaluated by 4 blinded pathologists. Measurements and Main Results: A total of 11 patients were enrolled in the monopolar and 10 in the CO2 laser group. There were no statistical difference in patients’ BMI, menopausal status, and tobacco use. Using the H&E stain, the mean extent of LTI in the monopolar group was significantly greater (1.1 mm) compared to the CO2 laser (0.7mm) ( p = .0191). Using the Masson’s Trichrome stain, the mean depth of coagulation effect using monopolar cautery was greater (327.7 microns) compared to the CO2 laser (290 microns) but not Conclusion: The use of CO2 laser during colpotomy resulted in less LTI compared with monopolar cautery. The impact of greater LTI on subsequent vaginal wound healing needs further evaluation.

title_normalized: potential genetic basis for failure of laparoscopic sacrocolpopexy a missense mutation in the zfyve16 gene
abstract_text: Study Objective: To determine whether a genetic basis exists for early multi-compartment pelvic organ prolapse recurrence after robotic-assisted laparoscopic sacrocolpopexy. Design: Case-control study. Setting: Hospital based practice. Patients: 10 robotic sacrocolpopexy patients who experienced early multi-compartment pelvic organ prolapse (POP) recurrence were cases. 40 robotic sacrocolpopexy patients who were known to be cured of POP at ≥ 12 months were controls. All patients were Caucasian. Measurements and Main Results: DNA from all cases and controls were isolated from buccal swabs and exome sequences were mapped to the Human Genome Reference Sequence (GRCh37) using the Ion Torrent Proton Semiconductor Sequencer (Life Technologies, Grand Island, NY). Variants in the Endofin (ZFYVE16) gene were compared between cases and controls. The variant dbSNP ID rs249038 (G/A) was found among 6 of our 10 cases and none of our controls. The remaining cases and all controls expressed the expected homozygous (G/G) pattern Conclusion: This missense mutation in the Endofin gene may play a significant role in development of surgical failure following POP surgery.

To examine our primary objective, we compared the average operating room associated costs for high versus low volume surgeons for each type of hysterectomy. title: Classification of Fallopian Tube Cytology to Develop a Model for Future Ovarian and Peritoneal Cancer Screening
authors_raw: M. Dhanani, A. Nassar, T. Dinh
title_normalized: classification of fallopian tube cytology to develop a model for future ovarian and peritoneal cancer screening
abstract_text: Study Objective: Recent findings indicate that many high grade serous ovarian and peritoneal neoplasms arise from the fallopian tube fimbria. The goal of this study is to develop a reproducible method for cytologic evaluation of fallopian tube cells that can adequately represent intraluminal pathology. Descriptive classification of the cytology was also performed. Design: Cytology samples were collected by brushing the fallopian tube fimbria with a cytobrush from patients undergoing salpingectomy for any indication, other than ovarian cancer. The cells were collected immediately after removal of the specimen and stored in SurePath liquid specimen cups, until slide preparation. The cytomorphology of the cells was described and correlated with histology findings. Setting: Specimens were collected at Mayo Clinic Florida from March to April 2014. Patients: Women undergoing clinically indicated salpingectomy for any diagnosis other than ovarian/peritoneal cancer and who gave informed consent were enrolled in the study. Measurements and Main Results: A total of 46 cytology samples were evaluated. Cellularity was moderate to high in 74% (34/46), with a clean background in 87% (40/46) and no artifact in 93% (43/46). All specimens had ciliated columnar cells and predominantly clustered and honeycomb architecture. Hyperchromatic nuclei were commonly present (78%), however nuclear pleomorphism and anisonucleosis was only associated with malignant cells. One out of the 46 samples contained malignant cells from a patient with endometrial malignancy Conclusion: Cells found throughout the fallopian tube can be collected from the fimbria and produce adequate yield for cytologic evaluation. A better understanding of cytomorphologic features may be used to correlate cytology with histologic diagnosis.

Since now strain analysis has been only been used to evaluate myocardial contractility. We correlated strain measurements in uteri that were previously described to have active and non-active uteri. Design: Prospective observational comparative study. Setting: Dutch non-university teaching hospital. Patients: 14 women with regular menstrual cycles, whose uteri were previously sonographically described as active and non-active uteri. Intervention: Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) followed by strain analysis. Measurements and Main Results: In 14 patients (9 with active (A); 4 non-active uterus (n-A)), 20 seconds of TVUS recordings were analyzed. Simultaneous measurement of strain in the cervix area revealed no activity and was used as control (p=0,008 A-group; p=0,068 in n-A group). Significant more activity (change in endometrial length) was noted in the A group (6 changes (2-11), compared to 1 active moment (range 0-7) in the n-A group (p=0.045). If alterations in size occurred, the active group showed a similar amplitude (0,91 Conclusion: Current transvaginal sonographic measurement of uterine contractions is subjective and time consuming. This feasibility study shows that transvaginal sonographic strain imaging is able to measure frequency, amplitude as well as velocity of uterine peristalsis. This technique might be able to easily measure uterine peristalsis for testing pre and post treatment effects (IVF, surgical interventions like myomectomy) in the future.

abstract_text: Study Objective: This study evaluated: 1) the feasibility of using a novel, completely handheld endometrial cryoablation device, 2) its intrauterine cavity ablation profile and characteristics (predictors of safety and efficacy), and 3) a perforation detection algorithm. Design: Clinical feasibility study. Setting: International gynecology practice. Patients: Fifteen women previously scheduled for a benign hysterectomy were consented for study. Intervention: With no endometrial pre-treatment for thinning (mechanical, hormonal, or cycle timing), the extirpated uteri were treated with a single, 150-second endometrial cryoablation procedure using nitrous oxide inside an endometrial liner deployed from a sub-5.5mm outer diameter device. The nitrous oxide was contained within a cylinder in the device’s handle. Intrauterine pressure was regulated by the device. The endomyometrial ablations were evaluated using macroscopic triphenyltetrazolium chloride Measurements and Main Results: The uteri were efficiently treated with no treatment-associated myometrial perforations. The corpus, bilateral cornua, and lower uterine segment endometrium and underlying myometrium were cryoablated (TTC-negative staining/non-viable). No TTC-negative lower endocervical or exocervical cryothermic injury was identified. Additionally, the device was able to detect extrauterine placement and shut off before the cryoablation was initiated. Conclusion: This novel device for endometrial cryoablation 1) provides cryoablation using a fully handheld device in a clinically appropriate timeframe (150 seconds), 2) has excellent cavity coverage with a contoured endometrial ablation, and 3) is able to detect placement outside of the uterus.

abstract_text: Study Objective: With the recent FDA warning against laparoscopic power morcellation necessitating larger incisions for leiomyoma extraction at the time of myomectomy, the objective of this study was to compare the trends over time and surgical outcomes of myomectomy by abdominal, mini-laparotomy, and traditional and robotic-assisted laparoscopy with and without tandem mini-laparotomy techniques. Design: Retrospective cohort study. Setting: Academic affiliated community hospital. Patients: Patients undergoing myomectomy between January 1995 and February 2015. Measurements and Main Results: 924 myomectomies were included. The average age was 38.2 ± 6.2 years and did not differ between groups. Abdominal myomectomy decreased from the primary technique in 1995 (95%) to less than 10% at the start of 2014, followed by a reverse in that trend over the last year. By 2003 traditional laparoscopy comprised 31% of cases and remained about 1/3 of cases until recently when there was a significant drop to 9% of cases in 2014 with a concomitant rise in combined laparoscopic and mini-laparotomy Conclusion: Over the past 20 years, we have seen a trend towards minimally invasive surgery with shorter hospitalizations and fewer complications. With recent changes in available techniques for myoma extraction in minimally invasive surgery, there has been a trend back to laparotomy or to employ the use of an additional mini-laparotomy incision; however, this study shows that this may not be benign with the potential for greater operative time, EBL, LOS, complications and blood transfusions.

Time required to close the incision was also compared. Design: Randomized controlled trial. Setting: Two academic affiliated hospitals. Patients: Women undergoing robotic surgery for benign indications by minimally invasive gynecologists at our institutions between 11/2012 and 10/2014 were enrolled in the study at their preoperative visit. Intervention: Patients were randomized to either a fascial closure device vs traditional fascial closure immediately prior to closure of the fascial incision. Pain score using a 10point Likert analogue pain scale were recorded on POD 1 and POD 14. Time to close the fascial incision, length of surgery, and BMI were also recorded. Measurements and Main Results: 63 patients were enrolled, 61 patients were randomized at the time of the fascial closure, with 2 patients enrolled converted to laparotomy. Multivariate analysis demonstrated that pain scores differed by fascial closure technique. Pain score on POD 1 was 3.41 vs 2.06 (p 0.024) in FCD vs TFC. Pain score on POD 14 was 1.82 vs 0.76 (p 0.027) in FCD vs TFC. Time to close fascia was 108.14s vs 145.36 (p 0.168) in FCD vs TFC. Conclusion: Our study demonstrates that the fascial closure device technique results in higher pain scores reported by patients without a significant difference in time to close the fascia.

abstract_text: Study Objective: The use of intracorporeal morcellation at the time of gynecologic surgery is under scrutiny due to concerns over the dissemination of occult uterine malignancies. We sought to determine the frequency of unsuspected sarcoma identified postoperatively in women undergoing surgery for benign gynecologic conditions at our institution. Design: Retrospective cohort study. Setting: Large academic institution. Patients: Patients undergoing hysterectomy for benign gynecologic indications from 2000-2014 at our institution were identified. Intervention: Patients who did not have a preoperative suspicion for malignancy and were found to have uterine sarcoma on pathology postoperatively were considered to have an occult uterine sarcoma. Relevant clinical and pathologic data were collected. Measurements and Main Results: 10,119 hysterectomies for benign gynecologic indications were performed during this period. Among these, 9 patients were found to have an unexpected uterine sarcoma, with an overall rate of 0.089%. These malignancies included 5 leiomyosarcomas (LMS), 2 endometrial stromal sarcomas (ESS), and 2 uterine adenosarcomas. Thus, in our population, the rate of unexpected LMS is 0.049%, 0.02% for ESS, and 0.02% for adenosarcomas. Cases included 5 total abdominal hysterectomies, 3 total vaginal Conclusion: In summary, occult uterine sarcoma occurs in approximately 0.09% of hysterectomies. The frequency is lower than the rate derived by the FDA in their pooled analysis. Institutional practices and regional referral patterns may impact local rates of occult sarcoma and should be considered during patient counseling and when developing clinical recommendations.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To evaluate detection rate (DR) of sentinel nodes (SLNs) in endometrial cancer patients submitted to Hysteroscopic injection of indocyanine green (ICG) and Laparoscopic near-infrared (L-NIR) fluorescence surgical treatment. Design: Prospectively collected data. Setting: Gynecologic oncology referral center. Patients: Consecutive patients with early stage endometrioid endometrial cancer scheduled for surgical treatment: total laparoscopic hysterectomy (TLH), bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO), SLNs mapping. Intervention: After laparoscopic tubal coagulation, patients were submitted to intraoperative hysteroscopic peritumoral injection of 5 mg of ICG followed by L-NIR fluorescence mapping, TLH and BSO. L-NIR procedures were performed using the Storz Professional Image Enhancement System (SPIES) with D-Light P light source (Karl Storz Endoskope GmbH &Co. KG, Tuttlingen, Germany). An upgrading of the light modulation, ameliorating background vision when NIR source was active, was performed after the first 16 Measurements and Main Results: A total of 32 procedures were performed. Mean age was 61 years (28-80) and mean BMI was 29.5 Kg/m2(19-43). At least one SLN was detected in the 84% of the whole population (27/32). When analyzing the first 16 patients compared to the latter ones, DR increased from 75% (12/16) to 93.7% (15/16). Mean number of detected SLNs was 3.3 (1-5) and in more than half of cases the aortic area mapped. Only one patient had nodal involvement with 3 positive SLNs. No adverse events were reported. Conclusion: The achieved DR for SLN mapping with ICG and L-NIR is comparable to both radioactive tracers series and ICG series with cervical injection, overcoming the usage of radioactive substances. Undoubtedly equipments permitting to identify and dissect nodes under NIR vision play an important role in increasing the DR and sparing surgical time. Further investigation is warranted on this topic.

These benefits were at the cost of longer operative time (weighted mean difference = 26.9min (95 % CI 8.08-45.82). The rate of intraoperative complications was Conclusion: LRH shows better short term outcomes compared with RH in patients with cervical cancer. The oncologic outcome and 5-year survival were similar between the two groups.

title: Hysteroscopic Metroplasty in Women With Unexplained Primary Infertility: A Prospective Cohort Study
authors_raw: T Shokeir, F Haroun

abstract_text: Study Objective: To evaluate the effect of hysteroscopic metroplasty as therapy for unexplained primary infertility in women with uterine septum as a sole cause for reproductive failure and to define the factors influencing reproductive success. Design: Prospective cohort study. Setting: University Teaching Hospital. Patients: We enrolled 103 infertile women with uterine septum as a sole cause for reproductive failure. All women had otherwise unexplained primary infertility >2 years duration and a follow-up >12 months. Uterine anomalies were diagnosed by hysterosalpingography (HSG) and transvaginal sonography (TVS). Diagnosis was further confirmed by office hysteroscopy. Intervention: Hysteroscopic metroplasty. Measurements and Main Results: Pregnancy rates (PR) according to patient and septum characteristics (septum size) were the main outcome measures. Follow-up was complete for 88 patients. Forty-two patients became pregnant (40.7%). The PR was significantly higher in women <35 years of age or with <3 years duration of unexplained primary infertility. Moreover, in women with a septum size larger than one-half of their uterine length the PR was significantly higher than those with septum size <1/2 of their uterus (P=.12). Conclusion: Fertility and pregnancy after hysteroscopic metroplasty in women with otherwise unexplained primary infertility and uterine septum as a sole cause for reproductive failure seems to depend on patient age, duration of infertility before metroplasty, and septum size. Women with a septum size larger than one-half of their uterine length have a higher chance of successful pregnancy after hysteroscopic metroplasty.

title: Comparison of Operative and Peri-Operative Results for Robotic and Laparoscopic Myomectomies
authors_raw: KJ Sasaki, CJ Steller, S Sulo, A Cholkeri-Singh, CE Miller
title_normalized: comparison of operative and peri operative results for robotic and laparoscopic myomectomies
abstract_text: Study Objective: To compare operative and peri-operative outcomes between laparoscopic and robotic myomectomies. Design: A review of prospectively maintained data in 2:1 matched cases of 96 laparoscopic and 48 robotic myomectomies. The cases were matched for number and size of fibroids removed, and timing of post-operative ultrasound. Medical charts were abstracted for demographics, operative and peri-operative outcomes. Setting: Specialty, suburban Chicago practice. Patients: Women over the age of 18 who underwent a laparoscopic or robotic myomectomy from July 2009 through December 2014. Intervention: Laparoscopic or robotic myomectomy. Measurements and Main Results: The data was analyzed via Student t-test for continuous variables and Chi-square or Fischer’s exact for categorical variables. A Spearman correlation was performed for conversion rate with estimated blood loss and operative time. A linear regression was performed for predictive variables on number of post-operative fibroids found. No differences were identified between the two cohorts with respect to age, pregnancy history, prior pelvic surgery, trocar number, or number or size of fibroids Conclusion: Laparoscopic and robotic myomectomies have similar outcomes in skilled surgical hands. The only difference was an increased conversion rate for complex cases in the robotic cohort. The number of post-operative fibroids did not differ, thus demonstrating that although robotics lacks haptic feedback, it does not limit a surgeon’s ability to identify and remove fibroids.

Design: Prospective Randomized Trial. Setting: University Hospital. Patients: Patients undergoing hysterectomy for benign indications. Intervention: One hundred forty four consenting patients were randomized to either robot-assisted or standard laparoscopic hysterectomy. Randomization occurred on the day of surgery. Operative times, intra-operative, and post-operative complications were recorded for twelve weeks. Measurements and Main Results: A total of 144 patients undergoing hysterectomy were randomized with 72 patients in each arm. Using Fisher’s exact tests and two-sample t-tests as appropriate, there was no evidence of differences between groups with respect to demographics. Estimated blood loss was not statistically significant between groups. abstract_text: Study Objective: To evaluate the effect of using a wireless audio headset device on operating room (OR) communication, efficiency and patient outcomes in robotic surgeries. Design: Prospective controlled trial. Setting: University affiliated, tertiary medical center in southeastern Michigan. Patients: Participants: All gynecologic and urologic OR staff participating in robotic procedures between January and March 2015. Intervention: During the 1 month control phase, all cases were performed without headsets, followed by the intervention phase where all team members used wireless headsets. Noise detecting devices were placed in each OR for both phases. At the end of each case, all team members evaluated the quality of communication, performance, teamwork and mental load using a 14-point questionnaire graded on a 1-10 scale. Higher overall scores indicated better communication and efficiency. Demographic data of OR team Measurements and Main Results: The study included 137 procedures, yielding 843 questionnaires for an overall response rate of 89% (843 of 943). The overall score of the questionnaire was significantly higher in cases where headsets were used (114.8±5.0 vs. 102.7±4.9; p<0.001). Noise intensity was lower in the study group compared to the control. For example, the fraction of time when noise level peaked above 70 dB with and without the headset was 6.0%±2.8 and 7.5%±3.6, respectively (p=0.003). There were no significant Conclusion: The use of wireless headset devices in robotic surgeries was associated with improved quality of communication between team members, as well as a reduction in the noise level in the OR.

title_normalized: computerized tomography adiposity morphometrics a novel approach to predict pulmonary intolerance in endometrial cancer patients undergoing robotic pelvic surgery rps
abstract_text: Study Objective: The physiologic stress of carbon dioxide pneumoperitoneum and steep trendelenburg affect the obese patient disproportionately and physiologic intolerance still limits the application of RPS in this population. The objective of the study was to identify morphometric characteristics of obese patients that best predict pulmonary tolerance to RPS using a novel method for quantifying adipose distribution. Design: Retrospective study. Setting: University Hospital. Patients: 51 endometrial cancer patients who underwent surgery to uniformly include robotic hysterectomy and lymphadenectomy between 2008 and 2014 and who had peri-operative computerized tomography (CT) imaging within 1 year of the surgery were identified. Intervention: Visceral fat volume (VFV) & subcutaneous fat volume (SFV) were quantified through average volume estimation of slices taken at 3 levels: mid waist, L2-L3 & L4-L5 in addition to waist circumference measurement using Intellispace Portal (ISP) software. Intraoperative physiologic data was recorded. Mean and maximum values were obtained for intraoperative physiologic data. Measurements and Main Results: The patients’ median body mass index (BMI) was 34.3 (range; 20-58.9). VFV & SFV quantified by CT at mid waist, L2-L3 & L4-L5 levels in addition to waist circumference were all significant independent predictors for peak airway pressures (PAP; average and maximum) and plateau airway pressures (PPlat; average and maximum) on multivariate regression analysis after adjustment for age, ethnicity, diabetes, hypertension, pulmonary disease, smoking, obstructive sleep apnea, ASA status and anesthesia Conclusion: The novel CT quantified morphometric measurements represent valid predictors of pulmonary tolerance to robotic surgery in the obese. Of the measures analyzed, visceral fat volume at L2-L3 best predicts pulmonary tolerance in the obese.

Patients: Advanced POP patients without complaining of urinary incontinence (UI) who underwent prolapse surgery from January 2011 to December 2013. Intervention: Preoperatively, a stress test, the 1-hour pad test and uroflowmetry were performed with prolapse reduction by a well-fitting ring pessary. All of the surgeries were performed by the same senior urogynecologist. Follow-up visits, scheduled at 3, 6 months and then annually, included a detailed (uro)gynecological history, clinical examination, and uroflowmetry in the clinic. Measurements and Main Results: Primary outcome was postoperative de novo UI. Median follow-up was 31 months (range 12-48 months). Of the 206 patients, 45 (21.8%) had evidence of occult SUI preoperatively, 62 (30.1%) exhibited de novo UI postoperatively, only 13 (6.3%) opted MUSs. Patients with occult SUI experienced higher de novo UI rate (53.3% versus 23.6%; RR=2.26, 95%CI: 1.25-4.25; P=0.000). The occult SUI and concomitant hysterectomy were identified as independent risk factors related to de novo UI (OR=4.19, 95%CI Conclusion: The preoperative 1-hour pad test with prolapse reduction is a feasible method for screening occult SUI, more than 1.9g may be a practical indicator of a postoperative subsequent MUI.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To estimate the prevalence and temporal trends in surgical management approach of ectopic pregnancy in the US, and to determine whether there are differences in rates or trends across important socio-demographic, clinical, and hospital-levels characteristics. Design: A cross-sectional analysis of the Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project’s Nationwide Inpatient Sample (HCUP-NIS), the largest all-payer inpatient database in the United States, was conducted for all patients who underwent surgical management of ectopic pregnancy. Joinpoint regression was used to estimate temporal trends (including the annual percent change [APC]) in salpingectomy versus salpingostomy during the study period and to evaluate whether trends were different across individual and Setting: All community hospitals in the US participating in HCUP, excluding rehabilitation and long-term acute care hospitals. Patients: The study includes all inpatient hospitalizations for women receiving treatment for ectopic pregnancy as identified using ICD-9-CM codes. Measurements and Main Results: During the study period, the number of inpatient discharges for ectopic pregnancy decreased from 36,574 in 1998 to 21,426 in 2011. Among tubal ectopic pregnancies, which is the most common type, the rate of salpingectomy increased steadily during the study period (APC=1.2%; 95%CI: 1.0, 1.3). Conversely, the rate of salpingostomy decreased moderately from 1998-2004 (APC= -3.7%; 95%CI: -6.3, -1.1), and then more drastically from 2004-2011 with a 10% annual decline (95%CI: -12.5, -6.6). There Conclusion: The rate of inpatient hospitalization for ectopic pregnancy has decreased in the US over the past 15 years. When hospitalization occurs, there is an increasing tendency to perform salpingectomy as oppose to salpingostomy as surgical management approach of ectopic pregnancy. There is no significant change in the trends for non-surgical management.

All women with Asherman Syndromen were operated in this centre using conventional instruments for hysterscopic adhesiolysis with fluoroscopic guidance. After succesfull adhesiolysis an IUD was placed and adjudant progesterone and oestrogen was given. Two months after surgery a second look hysterscopy was performed. Patients: Women who were diagnosed with Asherman Syndrome. Confirmation of intrauterine adhesions was done by hysteroscopy. All women underwend hysterscopic adhesiolysis with fluoroscopic guidance. Most of them had an amenorroe. Succes rate was defined as a normal uterine cavity with restore of normal menstrual bloodflow after adhesiolysis. This succeded in 95%. There was a five time increased change of a placental problem post partum. Conclusion: Patientes with Asherman Syndrome have a good chance of restoration of the intra uterine anatomy after adhesiolysis but have a high chance of recurrence of adhesions. If adhesiolysis is succesful the chance of a life birth is 68%. The chance of post partum placental problems is 5 times increased.

In 6/13 subjects abdominal muscle activity consistently preceded reported pain, suggesting menstrual pain could be referred from abdominal musculature. Women with pre-cramp abdominal muscle activity had a greater reduction in menstrual pain 90 Conclusion: Our results imply that prostaglandin synthesis leads to abdominal skeletal muscle cramping pain. abstract_text: Study Objective: The Robotic Training Network (RTN), a group of surgical educators, uses the Robotic-Objective Structured Assessment of Technical Skills (R-OSATS) to score dry lab robotic surgery drills. This is a valid and reliable method, but requires experts to evaluate each trainee. We sought to assess whether crowdsourcing (via Amazon Mechanical Turk) can be used as an alternative to expert evaluators for scoring dry lab surgical drills. Design: Method-comparison study. Setting: Training hospitals. Patients: As a part of a prior R-OSATS validation study, 105 resident, fellow, and expert robotic surgeons performed five dry lab tasks. These tasks were recorded, de-identified, uploaded to a private web-based location, and scored by three separate expert surgeons using R-OSATS. Intervention: We separated these previously recorded tasks into quartiles based on their mean R-OSATS scores. Representative recordings from the upper and lower scoring quartiles were used to train crowdworkers on R-OSATS scoring. After training, three new recordings from each quartile for each task were randomly selected. Crowdworkers assessed recorded drills using R-OSATS. Expert inter-rater reliability was calculated using intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC). Linear mixed effects models were used to Measurements and Main Results: Crowdworkers from Amazon Mechanical Turk (n=204) performed a total of 2,517 R-OSATS ratings within 16 hours. Expert surgeon R-OSATS scores had moderate internal-consistency (ICC=0.61, range 0.55-0.69 across tasks). Crowdworker R-OSATS ratings were highly correlated with expert ratings across each of the 5 dry lab tasks (figure, r range 0.75 - 0.91). Conclusion: Crowdsourced assessments of recorded dry lab surgical tasks using a validated assessment tool are a suitable alternative to faculty expert evaluation. Crowdsourcing would provide a rapid and accurate method of assessing technical skills of trainees while minimizing burdens on faculty time.

title: Learning Curve Analysis of Robot-Assisted Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy in a Gynecologic Oncology Practice at Winthrop University Hospital
authors_raw: S Sharma, E Chalas, E Jimenez, R Calixte
title_normalized: learning curve analysis of robot assisted total laparoscopic hysterectomy in a gynecologic oncology practice at winthrop university hospital
abstract_text: Study Objective: The primary objective is to determine the learning curve associated with performing a robot-assisted total laparoscopic hysterectomy. Design: Retrospective cohort analysis. Setting: Academic medical center. Patients: Two hundred women who underwent robot-assisted total laparoscopic hysterectomy at our institution from 2011-2014 by two gynecologic oncologists. Intervention: Data was collected on the first 100 cases performed by each surgeon by cross-referencing a log of operating room (OR) cases with a database of robotic surgeries performed by the surgeons of interest. The robotic console time represents the time taken to perform a robot-assisted total laparoscopic hysterectomy. Patient factors and operative details were accessed using scanned medical records through the Siemens health information system. Cases were recorded in a sequential fashion for each Measurements and Main Results: A total of 200 patients were included in the study. A learning curve for each surgeon was created using Basis-Spline (B-Spline) regression and cumulative sum (CUSUM) analysis. Points of proficiency and efficiency were identified for each surgeon. Both surgeons demonstrated similar points of proficiency at approximately 60 cases. For surgeon A, the point of efficiency was estimated at 82 cases. For surgeon B, the point of efficiency was estimated at 78 cases. Conclusion: As the points of proficiency and efficiency were similar for both surgeons, we believe this is an accurate preliminary representation of the learning curve of robot-assisted total laparoscopic hysterectomy. However, as robotic lymphadenectomy was also performed in 54% and 42% of the cases performed by surgeons A and B respectively, additional analysis is needed to clarify the learning curve.

Intervention: Of all 797 women who underwent surgical treatment for gynecologic disorders, 106 received endometrial cancer staging surgery, 59 received ovarian cancer staging surgery, 45 received radical hysterectomy for cervical cancer, 480 received complex total hysterectomy with or without adnexectomy and 107 received myomectomy. Patient demography, surgical outcomes and complications were evaluated. Measurements and Main Results: The median age and body mass index of patients were 46.9 years and 22.9 kg/m2. The median operative time, estimated blood loss, and postoperative hospital stay were 145.5 min, 139.6 mL, and 3.1 days, respectively. The overall complication rate was 5.9% and that only for oncologic cases was 8.6%. Intraoperative complications (n=6, 0.8%) consisted of four bowel laceration, one ureter laceration and one case of bladder injury. Early and late postoperative complications were 4.1% (n=33) and 1.0% Conclusion: Robotic-assisted laparoscopic surgery is a feasible approach in management of gynecologic disorders with an acceptable complication rate.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To develop and validate a preoperative ultrasound based staging system to predict laparoscopic level required for endometriosis surgery. Design: Multicenter prospective observational study. Setting: Tertiary referral laparoscopic unit. Patients: Consecutive women presenting with chronic pelvic pain +/- history of endometriosis from January 2009 to March 2015. Intervention: All women underwent a detailed specialized transvaginal ultrasound (TVS) to stage the endometriosis prior to laparoscopy. Two ultrasound staging systems (SS1 and SS2) were compared and correlated with the laparoscopic skill levels required (levels I - III as recommended by the Royal College of Obstetrics and Gynaecology). 200/267 were used as the training set to develop the staging system. Bootstrapping validation was performed on the total dataset of 267.. Ultrasound Staging Systems Measurements and Main Results: 267/274 (94%) women with pre-operative TVS and laparoscopic outcomes were included in the final analysis. The rate of correctly predicting the exact level of skills needed using SS1 and SS2 were 75.7% and 81.6% respectively. Cohen’s kappa agreement between SS1 and SS2 and levels of training required at surgery were 0.627 and 0.717, respectively. SS2 outperformed SS1 both in the training set and bootstrap validation. The sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive and negative predictive Conclusion: We have generated and validated a pre-operative ultrasound staging tool to triage women to the most appropriate level of laparoscopic endometriosis surgery. This staging system needs to be externally validated in order to assess its general applicability in other centres.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To determine whether the presence of transvaginal sonographic (TVS) soft markers (i.e. ovarian mobility, site specific tenderness (SST)) are significantly associated with endometriosis type and location at subsequent surgery. Design: Multicenter prospective observational study. Setting: Tertiary referral laparoscopic unit. Patients: All women with symptoms of chronic pelvic pain +/- history of endometriosis and a plan for laparoscopic endometriosis surgery from January 2009 to February 2013. Intervention: All women underwent a detailed history and specialized TVS prior to laparoscopy. During TVS, soft markers such as ovarian mobility and site-specific tenderness were assessed. Main outcome measures were the relationship between TVS soft markers (ovarian mobility, SST) and the following surgical findings: endometrioma, isolated peritoneal endometrosis (PE) (i.e. in the absence of deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE)/endometrioma/pouch of douglas(POD) obliteration), and posterior compartment DIE. Measurements and Main Results: 189/220 (86%) women with pre-operative TVS and laparoscopic outcomes were included in the final analysis. Unilateral/bilateral ovarian fixation was significantly associated with: unilateral/bilateral endometrioma, rectal and rectosigmoid DIE, POD obliteration, and the need for bowel surgery (all p<0.05). Right ovarian fixation was also significantly associated with right uterosacral ligament (USL) PE (p=0.03). When ovarian mobility was analysed in the absence of endometrioma (n=143), Conclusion: Ovarian fixation, in the presence or absence of endometrioma, appears to be a significantly associated with USL PE, posterior compartment DIE, POD obliteration and the need for bowel surgery. SST may be a potential TVS marker for pelvic sidewall PE, but does not appear to correlate with pelvic DIE location.

title: Ability of MRI and Ultrasound with Multidisciplinary Meeting to Pre Operatively Predict the Need for Bowel Resection
authors_raw: P Maher, T Ma, L Ellett, K Stone, ... E Readman
title_normalized: ability of mri and ultrasound with multidisciplinary meeting to pre operatively predict the need for bowel resection
abstract_text: Study Objective: To determine if pre-operative endometriosis focused ultrasound (US) and Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) combined with multidisciplinary meeting (MDM) improves the prediction for need for bowel resection at time of surgery compared to US or MRI alone. Design: Prospective cohort study. Setting: Tertiary gynaecology referral hospital. Patients: Patients attending the Endosurgical unit who had surgery completed from January 2012 to January 2015 for management of stage IV endometriosis. Intervention: Predicted rates of need for bowel resection were calculated for routine management (US, MRI and MDM review) versus MRI or US alone as predicted by 2 senior surgeons on the unit. These rates were then compared against the ‘Ideal’ rate of bowel resection- defined as cases where bowel resection was performed, need for second procedure with bowel resection or need to call for colorectal surgeon intraoperatively. Measurements and Main Results: 35 cases were eligible for inclusion. US alone had an average sensitivity of 78%, specificity of 68%, PPV 68%, NPV 78%, accuracy 73%, Fisher exact test p=0.0006-0.0866 for the prediction of bowel resection. MRI alone had an average sensitivity of 51%, specificity of 92%%, PPV 88%, NPV 69%, accuracy 72%, Fisher exact test p=0.0049-0.0064 for the prediction of bowel resection. Conclusion: US, MRI and MDM review performs better than MRI or US alone in the prediction of need for bowel resection during surgery for stage IV endometriosis. This results in better allocation of limited theatre resources and colorectal surgical time. The multidisciplinary review also allows all clinicians to benefit from each other’s experience and specialist knowledge which is expected to continue to improve prediction for bowel resection.

abstract_text: Study Objective: Computed tomographic colonography (CTC) is the radiological examination of choice for colorectal cancer screening. Setting: University Teaching Hospital. Patients: Participants were recruited among women with strong suspicion of intestinal endometriosis based on symptoms and clinical examination. Individuals were excluded from the study if they had history of colorectal surgery, contraindications to bowel preparation or CTC. Intervention: CTC was performed using a 16-section multidetector CT scanner. Fecal tagging was performed. The colon was dilated inflating room air. The raw data were transferred to a workstation with a dedicated VC software package (General Electric ADW 4.2.5). The 3D endo-luminal fly-through and virtual dissection reconstructions allowed visualization of the bowel lumen. Patients underwent surgery within 2 months from CTC. Radiological images were compared with surgical and pathologic findings. Efficacy Measurements and Main Results: Among the 53 patients enrolled in this study, 42 underwent surgery and were included in the final analyses. 24 patients (57.1%) had bowel endometriosis at surgery. 10 nodules were located on the sigmoid, 9 nodules were located on the rectum and 5 on the rectosigmoid junction. The mean (± SD) diameter of the bowel nodules was 25.8 (± 5.8) mm. The sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative predictive values, positive and negative likelihood ratios of CTC in diagnosing bowel endometriosis Conclusion: CTC has high sensitivity and moderate specificity in the diagnosis of recto-sigmoid endometriosis; the exam was well tolerated by the patients. These results should be confirmed in trials with larger sample size.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To classify types of uterine endometriosis and identify its incidence in patients who underwent pelvic clearance for severe recto-vaginal endometriosis. Setting: The Royal Surrey County Hospital, Guildford, UK, a tertiary referral center for minimally invasive gynecological surgery and endometriosis. Patients: Patients who had surgery performed for severe endometriosis were invited to participate in the study. Questionnaires were completed (Endometriosis Health Profile (EHP)-30, Gastro Intestinal Quality of Life Index (GIQLI), EQ-5D and Visual Analogue Scores (VAS) for chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea, dyspareunia and dyschezia. Those who did not have fertility desires were offered a pelvic clearance in addition to excising all endometriosis. Questionnaires were completed pre operatively and up to Intervention: In those where conservative surgery was performed all endometriosis was excised including the rectal nodule (this was performed either as a rectal shave, disc resection or segmental bowel resection). The study was carried out from 2004 to present and the first 99 patients that completed the study up to 12 months post operatively were included. Measurements and Main Results: Significant improvement in symptoms and scores was seen in both the conservative group and in those that underwent pelvic clearance (P<0.01), as compared to pre surgery. However, those in the pelvic clearance group had significantly improved scores compared to those in the conservative group (P<0.01). When analyzing the specimens it was clear that 80% had uterine pathology; adenomyosis (40%), serosal endometriosis (14%) and subserosal endometriosis (26%). Conclusion: This may explain why women who have clearance surgery for severe recto-vaginal endometriosis do better than those who have conservative surgery.

Intervention: Appendectomy during laparoscopic (n=59) or robotic (n=50) surgery. Measurements and Main Results: A normal appendix was found in 29 cases (26.6%), endometriosis was found in 30 cases (27.6%) and a condition other than endometriosis was found in 50 cases (45.8%): fibrous obliteration (n=21/19.2%), lymphoid hyperplasia (n=20/18.3%), fibrosis (n=5/4.6%), acute appendicitis (n=1/0.9%), carcinoid tumor (n=1/0.9%), melanosis (n=1/0.9%) and hyperplastic polyp (n=1/0.9%). abstract_text: Study Objective: To improve resident competence in managing laparoscopic vascular injuries via simulation. Design: Prospective cohort study of obstetrics and gynecology residents who underwent a laparoscopic vascular injury educational module with simulation. Residents took a pre-test, an immediate post-test, and a late post-test two weeks later. The tests assessed perception, knowledge, and confidence and contained survey questions, multiple-choice questions, and a free-text portion to simulate dictation. Paired t-test was used to analyze continuous variables. Setting: Academic medical center. Patients: Obstetrics and gynecology residents at our medical center in April 2014. Intervention: Residents participated in an educational module which included a simulated laparoscopic procedure complicated by a major vascular injury. A mannequin containing basic vascular anatomy allowed for bleeding to occur. Measurements and Main Results: Among the twelve residents who participated, two (17%) had ever been involved in a vascular injury and two (17%) had ever received prior instruction on management of vascular injury. Knowledge scores improved from 48% on the pre-test to 88% on the early post-test (p<0.001) and 74% on the late post-test (p=0.002). Qualitative dictation scores improved from 11% on the pre-test to 43% on the early post-test (p=0.005) and 30% on the late post-test (p=0.009). Resident confidence improved from 4% on Conclusion: Our residents rarely see laparoscopic vascular injuries or learn about their management. This simulation showed a meaningful, statistically significant, and durable improvement in resident knowledge, confidence, and dictation quality. It was widely accepted by the residents and unanimously chosen to be part of the annual curriculum.

title: A New Virtual Reality Training Module for Laparoscopic Surgical Skills and Equipment Handling: Can Multitasking Be Trained? A Randomized Controlled Trial
authors_raw: PJ Bongers, D Van Hove, LP Stassen, J Dankelman, HW Schreuder
title_normalized: a new virtual reality training module for laparoscopic surgical skills and equipment handling can multitasking be trained a randomized controlled trial
abstract_text: Study Objective: During laparoscopic surgery distractions often occur and multitasking between surgery and other tasks, such as technical equipment handling, is a necessary competence. In psychology reduction of adverse effects of distraction is demonstrated when specifically multitasking is trained. Aim of this study is to examine whether multitasking can be trained in a virtual reality (VR) laparoscopic skills simulator. Design: Prospective randomized study. Setting: University Medical Center. Patients: Medical students (n=42)were randomly assigned to either intervention or control group. Intervention: A new integrated training module for the Simendo® Virtual Reality simulator was developed. The insufflator training and laparoscopic skills exercises were combined into one training module. The control group trained both modules separately. The intervention group trained the integrated module in which multitasking was a required competence. At random moments problems with the insufflator appeared and forced the trainee to multitask. During repetitions of a different multitask VR skills exercise Measurements and Main Results: All participants performed better in the post-test exercises without distraction of the insufflator compared to the exercises in which multitasking was necessary to solve the insufflator problems. After training the intervention group was significantly quicker in solving the insufflator problems (mean 1,60 Log(seconds) versus 1,70 Log(seconds), p=0,02). No significant differences between both groups were seen in laparoscopy time and errors. Conclusion: Multitasking has negative effects on the laparoscopic performance. This study suggests an additional learning effect of training multitasking in VR laparoscopy simulation, because the trainees are able to handle a secondary task (solving insufflator problems) quicker. These results may aid the further development of laparoscopy VR training programs.

abstract_text: Study Objective: Laparoscopic management of ectopic pregnancy is a common procedure encountered during Obstetrics and Gynecology (Ob-Gyn) residency training. Although a limited number of commercial products exist to aid simulation in teaching laparoscopic salpingostomy and salpingectomy, these models are either incomplete (ex. partial tube only) or quite expensive, thus limiting application. Our goal was to develop a low fidelity uterine model with simulated ectopic pregnancy that could be used by Ob-Gyn Design: Pilot design of a novel low fidelity uterine model with simulated ectopic pregnancy for use in laparoscopic simulation. Setting: The model is manipulated with traditional laparoscopic instruments within a standard laparoscopic task trainer. Patients: Ob-Gyn residents at a single academic center used the created model when performing simulation for laparoscopic salpingostomy and salpingectomy. Intervention: A low fidelity uterine model with simulated ectopic pregnancy was constructed using common items. A plastic maraca functioned as the uterus with attached uterine manipulator. A plastic whoosh ball placed inside a small balloon served as the ectopic pregnancy; this was placed inside a larger balloon and attached to the maraca to simulate fallopian tube with ectopic pregnancy. Additional balloons were added for ovaries and press and seal was used as peritoneum. To conserve resources, “ectopic Measurements and Main Results: Eight uterine models with simulated ectopic pregnancy were constructed for approximately $16 total. The models functioned well within the laparoscopic box trainer, and all residents (n=16) successfully performed laparoscopic salpingostomy, followed by salpingectomy. Feedback from residents regarding the model was positive. Conclusion: Our low fidelity uterine model with simulated ectopic pregnancy can easily be constructed for minimal cost and used in residency training programs to practice laparoscopic treatment of ectopic pregnancy in a simulated environment.

authors_raw: F Moiety, H Salem, E Oraby

abstract_text: Study Objective: To evaluate the use of antibiotics in preventing post-procedure infection in diagnostic laparoscopy and office hysteroscopy. To analyze the financial impact of antibiotic prophylaxis for minor endoscopic procedures at the hospital level. Design: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Setting: Tertiary Gynecology Hospital. Patients: 280 patients undergoing endoscopic procedures were included. They were randomly allocated into 2 groups based on receiving antibiotic prophylaxis versus placebo. Intervention: Office hysteroscopy were indicated for 140 patients, while the remaining 140 underwent diagnostic laparoscopy. Measurements and Main Results: Development of clinical or laboratory signs of infection over a period of follow up of 6 weeks was measured. None of the cases who underwent laparoscopy developed signs related to infection except for one case in placebo group who developed superficial wound infection (1.4% of the placebo group and 0.71% of the whole laparoscopy group). After Conclusion: Rate of risk-reducing salpingectomy increased substantially over the course of the study period. title: Can Narrowband Imaging Improve the Laparoscopic Identification of Superficial Endometriosis? A Prospective Cohort Trial
authors_raw: TJ Ma, L Ellett, K McIlwaine, J Manwaring, ... P Maher
title_normalized: can narrowband imaging improve the laparoscopic identification of superficial endometriosis a prospective cohort trial
abstract_text: Study Objective: To assess if the use of Narrow Band Imaging (NBI) at diagnostic laparoscopy improves the detection of superficial endometriosis. Design: Prospective cohort study. Setting: Laparoscopic specialty unit within a tertiary teaching hospital. Patients: Patients undergoing laparoscopy for investigation and treatment of pelvic pain who have no pre-operative ultrsound evidence of of deep infiltrating endometriosis. Intervention: Standard inspection of the pelvis was performed using white light with areas suspicious for endometriosis documented. Repeat evaluation of the pelvis was then performed using NBI with any additional areas of suspicion documented. All areas identified as possible endometriosis were surgically excised with monopolar diathermy and assessed for histopathology. Measurements and Main Results: 53 eligible patients undergoing laparoscopy for pelvic pain were included. Thirty two (60%) patients had at least one lesion suspicious for endometriosis excised after white light survey of the pelvis. Twenty four (75% of patients biopsied) had endometriosis confirmed on histopathology. NBI survey revealed an additional area of suspicion in 11 patients, of which six (54%) of these additional samples were positive for endometriosis. Conclusion: NBI not only made neovascularization of superficial endometriosis more prominent but the depth of field of the laparoscopy was significantly shortened. This forced the operator to make a very close inspection of the peritoneal surfaces. Therefore NBI is a simple and non-invasive tool that can aid in the diagnosis of superficial endometriosis that may otherwise be missed at standard white light laparoscopy.

abstract_text: Study Objective: 1. Develop a grading tool to objectively assess various components of the laparoscopic myomectomy procedure. 2. Setting: Multiple fully-affiliated academic hospitals. Patients: Patients that had a laparoscopic or robotic myomectomy over a three-year period (2012-2015) for which a video of the myomectomy was made were included. Intervention: Two gynecologists independently reviewed laparoscopic/robotic myomectomy surgical videos. Reviewers were blinded to whether or not patients had received pre-treatment with UPA. Each procedure was scored using a new 22-point surgical experience grading tool. The tool contained 6 subscales that evaluated: depth of myometrial incision, ease of myoma-myometrium cleavage plane identification, ease of myoma detachment, blood loss during fibroid detachment, myometrial blood loss after fibroid Measurements and Main Results: 50 myomectomy procedure videos were assessed. There was high agreement between reviewers for the total surgical experience score (intra-class correlation 0.97, p<0.01). Agreement for the six subscales of the tool ranged between 0.80 – 0.98. UPA pre-treatment was used in 25 procedures (50%) compared to 25 (50%) who received no pre-treatment. There was no difference in mean surgical experience score in the UPA group (13.5 ± 2.9) compared to the group without pre-treatment (13.3 ± 2.9), p=0.81. Conclusion: The new scale of surgical experience at laparoscopic myomectomy had high inter-rater reliability. authors_raw: X Guan, Y Wang, J Gisseman

abstract_text: Background: Single-incision laparoscopic hysterectomy can be difficult because of longer surgery time, steep learning curve, and the need for articulated instruments, but is especially challenging in patients with a uterus larger than 20 cm. Objective: To describe the single incision laparoscopic technique with an articulated energy device for uterus larger than 20 cm. Clinical information: A 49 year-old G3P3 female with 24 weeks sized fibroid uterus requested supracervical hysterectomy presented with a 2-year history of pelvic pain and menorrhagia. abstract_text: Objectives: The aim of this study is to clarify cases with urinary tract injury during TLH, and to review strategies for repairing urinary tract complications. Methods: All 2604 women who underwent TLH in our department from January 2011 to December 2015 were included in this study. Results: The ureter was injured in 8 cases (0.31%), the bladder was perforated in 7 cases (0.27%), and the bladder muscle layer was injured in 27 cases (1.04%). During the study period, no cases needed conversion to laparotomy to repair urinary tract complications. I will present some cases of urinary tract complications and explain how these complications were managed. Conclusions: It is most important that we strive to not cause urinary tract complications in the first place. However, when they do occur, appropriate treatment with manipulation can prevent conversion to laparotomy.

Factors most influential for decreased or discontinued use included patient refusal, FDA statement release and change of institutional policy. Men were more likely than women to decrease or discontinue use (58.46% vs 19.35%, p=.0015). There were no Conclusion: Although attitudes towards its use are divided, there seems to be an overall consensus that minimally invasive techniques can be sustained without the use of power morcellation. title: Uterine Synechiae After Hysteroscopic Myomectomy: Should We Use Bipolar or Monopolar Energy?
authors_raw: H Fernandez, P Capmas, A Nazac
title_normalized: uterine synechiae after hysteroscopic myomectomy should we use bipolar or monopolar energy
abstract_text: Study Objective: To compare synechiae rate after bipolar hysteroscopic myomectomy versus monopolar hysteroscopic myomectomy. Design: Unicentric randomized controlled trial. Setting: Gynecology unit of a teaching hospital. Patients: Women 18 to 42 years old, with heavy menstrual bleeding or infertility and type 0, 1 or 2 myoma. Intervention: Hysteroscopic myomectomy was performed by a rigid resectoscope with monopolar or bipolar energy 26 Fr brand Karl Storz® through an electrode with a cutting loop of 4mm. The patients were operated in the follicular phase. No anti adhesions device should not be inserted in the cavity: neither anti adhesions gel or other mechanical device in the cavity. A diagnostic hysteroscopy was performed 6 weeks after surgery. Measurements and Main Results: 58 women were randomized for type of energy: monopolar (n = 27) vs. bipolar (n = 31). No significant difference was found between the two groups regarding patient characteristics. Mean number of myoma was 1.4 ± 0.8 myoma. The localization of the myoma was mainly anterior (n = 26) or posterior (n = 23). Myomas were mainly type 1 (n = 23) or 2 (n = 33) out of the 69 resected myoma. Conclusion: This study is the first randomized trial about rate of synechia after hysteroscopic myoma resection using monopolar or bipolar energy. Bipolar energy led to significantly less synechiae after hysteroscopic resection of type 0 to 2 myoma compared to monopolar energy. Therefore, we recommend the use of bipolar energy and office hysteroscopic control 6 weeks after surgery.

There were outliers for the gene expression in all groups, but with no clinical Conclusion: Current scientific evidence shows that endometrial polyps presenting with postmenopausal bleeding are at greater risk for malignancy, but there is a lack of studies to define whether the polyps are in fact cancer precursors, or only a marker that allows earlier diagnosis of malignancy.

Patients: Three different cohorts were included: 1) surgical novices (no robotic/laparoscopic experience) 2) surgical senior residents and fellows (intermediate robotic/laparoscopic experience) and 3) established laparoscopic/robotic surgeons. Intervention: Each study group completed three dry lab exercises that are essential components of the Fundamentals of Laparoscopic Surgery (FLS) curriculum, on a standard laparoscopic box trainer and in a robotic dry lab set-up. Measurements and Main Results: Performance was primarily measured as time to completion, with adjustments based on errors. Means of performance for the dominant versus non-dominant hand for each task were calculated and compared with a paired t-test. A total of 36 subjects were enrolled study-wide (12/group). Overall execution time for all three tasks with the dominant hand was significantly different from that with the non-dominant hand in the laparoscopic setting (439.4s vs. 568.4s respectively; p=0.004) while the Conclusion: Robot-assisted laparoscopy eliminates the operative handedness observed in conventional laparoscopy, allowing for virtual ambidexterity at all levels of surgical training. Further investigation is warranted in order to define the ultimate clinical value of this particular robotic attribute with respect to actual operative outcomes and patient safety in the surgical learning curve.

Setting: Large teaching hospital with a tertiary referral function for patients with gynecological prolapse. Patients: All patients (N= 165 ) undergoing RASC between 2011-2013 were enrolled. Median follow-up was 12 months. The POP-Q improved significantly (p<0.001) for all four anatomic landmarks. Urinary incontinence (UDI-6 score 4,2 vs 2,4 p<0,0005) scores improved significantly at 12 months. The quality Conclusion: This is the largest cohort, reporting prospectively on RASC. At one year follow-up the POP-Q for all four anatomic landmarks and the urinary incontinence scores improved significantly. A positive effect on the quality of life and dyspareunia was observed. No mesh erosions were seen.. abstract_text: Study Objective: To compare individual and traditional biofeedback combined with electrostimulation therapy on three aspects: myodynamia,pelvic organ prolapse (POP) and sexual life. authors_raw: M Mára, Z Lisá, K Hlinecká, D Kuzel
title_normalized: peri procedural pregnancy and peripartum complications in patients with laparoscopic or open myomectomy
abstract_text: Study Objective: Myomectomy has traditionally been presented as a risk factor in perinatology, especially due to the risk of uterine rupture. Our goal was to evaluate the frequency of serious peri-procedural, pregnancy and perinatal complications of women with laparoscopic (LM) or open myomectomy (OM). Design: Prospective cohort single-institution study with duration of follow-up between 1 and 14 years. Setting: Center for Gynecological Endoscopy and Minimally Invasive Surgery in tertiary care, University Teaching Hospital. Patients: All women with reproductive plans and symptomatic intramural uterine fibroid/s larger than 4 cm treated with LM or OM in our center were prospectively followed. Patients were instructed to wait with conception for at least 6 months following myomectomy. In case of gestation the course of pregnancy and labor have been analyzed with focus on clinical complications. Intervention: Laparoscopic or open myomectomy. Measurements and Main Results: 1452 women (1224 LM and 228 OM) entered the study between the years 2002 and 2015. To our best knowledge 546 pregnancies and 352 deliveries (including 12 twins) have occurred in 424 post-myomectomy women. The incidence of complications was as following: 1.9% of unexpected histological result (mostly smooth muscle tumors with uncertain malignant potential), 0.8% of surgical revisions, 20.4% of abortions, 1.2% of ectopic pregnancies (all tubal), 80.2% of Caesarian sections (mostly elective), Conclusion: Our results show that the incidence of major obstetric complications following myomectomy does not exceed their frequency in normal population and that the risk of the uterine rupture seems to be very low.

Patients: 42 patients with the uterine scar incompetence were enrolled in the study. Intervention: Patients were divided in 4 groups for intervention: 1st group: diagnostic hysteroscopy and laparoscopy (9); 2nd group: hysteroscopy, laparoscopic repair of incompetence scar (12); 3rd group: hysteroscopy, laparoscopic excision of incompetence scar margins and its repair (18); 4th group: diagnostic laparoscopy and hysteroscopyc resection, coagulation (3). Measurements and Main Results: Mean age of patients was 29,6+/-3,9 years. 88,1% patients had a history of urgent cesarean section. The thickness of the scar by ultrasound and MRI measured up to 4 mm; the presence of niches was found in all cases. 38 patients had menorrhagia, metrorrhagia, pain, dyspareunia, infertility. Mean operative time was 38+/-12; 109+/-36; 132[+/-32; 51+/-14; min, respectively. Intraoperative blood loss was less than 50 ml. No complications were observed. One patient in group 3 underwent reoperation. Conclusion: Laparoscopic repair of incompetence scar is a minimally invasive and effective treatment. But this issue requires further study: development of criteria for the evaluation of the scar incompetence with the use of ultrasound and MRI, indications for incompetence scar repair, the choice of surgical treatment, and evaluation of results.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To assess whether there is a difference in the genotype for the gene encoding COMT in women with vulvodynia compared to patients without vulvodynia since mutations of COMT have been associated with higher pain sensitivity. Design: Prospective case-control study. Setting: Subjects were recruited from three pelvic pain clinics across the United States. Patients: Women were recruited as cases if they had vulvar or vestibular pain of at least 3 months duration in absence of obvious pathology (infection, inflammation, malignancy, neuropathic). Women were included as controls if they had no vulvar or vestibular pain and had never had a period of painful intercourse for 3 months or longer. A total of 494 women were enrolled in the study: 220 women with vulvodynia and 274 controls. Measurements and Main Results: Buccal swabs for DNA testing were collected from both patients with vulvodynia and controls. The DNA was tested for presence of genetic polymorphisms of the COMT gene. The L allele is associated with a COMT mutation that causes heightened pain sensitivity, whereas the H allele is the wild-type form. There was no significant difference between vulvodynia and controls in prevalence of L,L genotype and actually the H,H genotype was more prevalent in the vulvodynia group (31.4%) compared to the Conclusion: Higher pain sensitivity in certain vulvodynia populations may be associated with COMT mutations, but overall we found no difference in the prevalence of the mutated form of COMT among women with and without vulvodynia.

abstract_text: Study Objective: To examine the correlation between frequency of urinary symptoms and clinical suspicion indexes of interstitial cystitis (IC) as well as cystoscopic findings after cystoscopy with hydrodistension (CwHD). Design: Prospective interventional cohort study with blinded image review. Setting: Participants were recruited from an academic urogynecologic and minimally invasive gynecologic practice. Patients: All participants in the study are patients scheduled to have routine gynecologic or urogynecologic procedures involving cystoscopy. 224 of the 269 women initially enrolled in this study are included in this interim analysis. All 224 participants had complete data sets and met all criteria. Intervention: Patients scheduled for cystoscopy or cystoscopy with hydrodistension were all consented for CwHD. Participants completed questionnaires including the IC Symptom Index (ICSI). Originally scheduled procedure (cystoscopy vs CwHD) and treating physician expectancy of IC served as clinical suspicion indexes. A panel of three urogynecologists evaluated de-identified picture sets. Measurements and Main Results: Statistical analysis comparing ICSI components with indexes of IC was performed using SPSS version 23.0 for Windows. There is significant correlation between ICSI bladder burning/pain/discomfort component and indexes other than image review diagnosis (initially scheduled procedure P<0.001, and physician expectancy P<0.001, physician diagnosis P <0.01, image review diagnosis P=0.28). Urinary frequency and urgency also reached statistical significance for association with clinical suspicion Conclusion: We found a strong relationship of frequency of burning/pain/discomfort score with suspicion indexes and physician diagnosis of IC, but not image review diagnosis. In this patient population with high prevalence of urinary symptoms, individual urinary symptoms and ICSI score correlate poorly with cystoscopic findings. Better diagnostic tools are needed for interstitial cystitis and this ongoing study is aiming to address that need.
